questions.explanation: Tracheids and vessels are the main water and mineral conducting tissues of xylem. Tracheid has pointed tips but vessels have blunt ends.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Anatomy of Flowering Plants

questions.question: Epidermal appendages, unicellular or multicellular outgrowth strictly epidermal in origin is_________.
questions.question_raw: Epidermal appendages, unicellular or multicellular outgrowth strictly epidermal in origin is_________.
questions.explanation: Trichomes are epidermal appendages, unicellular or multicellular outgrowth strictly epidermal in origin present on the stem of plants.

questions.question: In angiosperms, vascular tissue develops from:
questions.question_raw: In angiosperms, vascular tissue develops from:
questions.explanation: In angiosperms, vascular tissue develops from plerome. Plerome forms the central core of the primary meristem of a plant or plant part.

questions.question: Which epidermis of the leaf contains more stomata?
questions.question_raw: Which epidermis of the leaf contains more stomata?

options.label: A
options.text: Both adaxial and abaxial contain same

questions.explanation: Stomata is present more on the lower surface of the leaf. The lower surface of the leaf is called abaxial part of the leaf that is away from direct sunlight to prevent an excess of water loss.

questions.question: Jute fibres deteriorate because they have _______.
questions.question_raw: Jute fibres deteriorate because they have _______.
questions.explanation: Jute fibres are made of a high amount of lignin that deteriorates in the presence of high moisture and water.

questions.question: In monocotyledons, guard cell of stomata are:
questions.question_raw: In monocotyledons, guard cell of stomata are:
questions.explanation: In monocotyledons, the guard cell of stomata is dumb-bell shaped, thicker on top and bottom and thinner in the middle.

questions.question: Which one functions as a Biological check post?
questions.question_raw: Which one functions as a Biological check post?
questions.explanation: Endodermis forms the outermost layer of vascular bundles. Anything entering inside the plant body must cross the endodermis to be transported via xylem and phloem. So, endodermis is called a biological check post of the cell.

questions.question: Which part of epidermis of the leaf contains fewer stomata?
questions.question_raw: Which part of epidermis of the leaf contains fewer stomata?

options.label: A
options.text: Both adaxial and abaxial contain same

questions.explanation: There are a number of differences between abaxial and adaxial stomata. First, stomatal density is usually higher on the abaxial surface than it is on the adaxial surface of leaves.

questions.question: The Casparian thickening occurs in the cells of:
questions.question_raw: The Casparian thickening occurs in the cells of:
questions.explanation: The tangential, as well as radial walls of the endodermal cells, have a deposition of water-impermeable, waxy material-suberin-in the form of Casparian strips.

questions.question: The vascular bundles are closed when they:
questions.question_raw: The vascular bundles are closed when they:
questions.explanation: In the monocotyledons, the vascular bundles have no cambium present in them. Hence, since they do not form secondary tissues they are referred to as closed.

questions.question: In leaves, the ground tissue consists of thin-walled chloroplast containing cells are called _________.
questions.question_raw: In leaves, the ground tissue consists of thin-walled chloroplast containing cells are called _________.
questions.explanation: The tissues of a leaf that are located in between the layers of the epidermis and carry on photosynthesis, consisting of the palisade layer and the spongy parenchyma. Most mesophyll cells contain chloroplasts.

questions.question: Which of the following do not undergo any secondary growth?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following do not undergo any secondary growth?
questions.explanation: Secondary growth in monocotyledonous roots is not observed as cambium is absent between xylem and phloem in a vascular bundle.

questions.question: The shape of guard cells in grasses is:
questions.question_raw: The shape of guard cells in grasses is:
questions.explanation: Dumb-bell-shaped guard cells are found in grasses. Dumb-bell shaped guard cells are more advanced in evolutionary terms and more efﬁcient physiologically because guard cells of grasses require fewer solutes and less water to achieve a given unit increase in the aperture​.

questions.question: Monocotyledonous leaves are_________.
questions.question_raw: Monocotyledonous leaves are_________.
questions.explanation: Monocotyledonous leaves are isobilateral as both the lower and upper sides of the leaves are the same.

questions.question: The main seat of photosynthesis in the leaf is:
questions.question_raw: The main seat of photosynthesis in the leaf is:
questions.explanation: The palisade parenchymatous cell in the mesophyll region has numerous chloroplasts. Hence, it is the main seat of photosynthesis in leaf.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Anatomy of Flowering Plants

questions.question: Reticulate venation is characteristic in:
questions.question_raw: Reticulate venation is characteristic in:
questions.explanation: Reticulate venation: In this type, the veins branch and unite forming a complicated network, All the veins are interconnected, like the strands of the net. Leaves of ‘Dicot plants’ generally have reticulate venation e.g., Hibiscus, Redgram.

questions.question:
Select the correct statement.
 Monocot roots do not undergo secondary growth.
 The hypodermis is a sclerenchymatous cell in dicot stems.
 Monocot roots undergo secondary growth.
 The hypodermis is collenchymatous cell in monocot stems.
questions.question_raw:
Select the correct statement.

  1. Monocot roots do not undergo secondary growth.
  2. The hypodermis is a sclerenchymatous cell in dicot stems.
  3. Monocot roots undergo secondary growth.
  4. The hypodermis is collenchymatous cell in monocot stems.
questions.explanation: The roots of extant vascular cryptogams and most monocotyledons do not show any secondary growth; they remain entirely primary throughout their life.

questions.question: Bulliform or motor cells are present in
questions.question_raw: Bulliform or motor cells are present in
questions.explanation: Bulliform cells or motor cells are large, bubble-shaped epidermal cells that occur in groups on the upper surface of the leaves of many monocots on the upper surface of the leaf.

questions.question: In arborescent monocots stem, a secondary cambium grows in the following type of vascular bundle is seen in
questions.question_raw: In arborescent monocots stem, a secondary cambium grows in the following type of vascular bundle is seen in
questions.explanation: It is the anatomy of monocotyledonous root.

questions.question: Anatomy of stem differs from the root in:
questions.question_raw: Anatomy of stem differs from the root in:

options.label: A
options.text: Cutinized epidermal wall, conjoint vascular bundle, and narrow cortex

options.label: B
options.text: Stem hairs, absence of chloroplast an broad cortex

options.label: C
options.text: Secondary vascular growth and presence of stomata

options.label: D
options.text: Xylem exarch, vascular bundle radial and narrow cortex

correct_option_texts: Cutinized epidermal wall, conjoint vascular bundle, and narrow cortex
questions.explanation: The stem shows the epidermis covered with a thin layer of cuticle, narrow cuticle, and each vascular bundle is conjoint, open, and is endarch protoxylem.

questions.question: The stem of grasses and related plants elongate by the activity of:
questions.question_raw: The stem of grasses and related plants elongate by the activity of:
questions.explanation: Grasses belong to monocotyledonous angiosperms in which secondary growth does not occur. Plants elongate by the activity of both apical and intercalary meristem.

questions.question: Separate xylem and phloem bundles are known as_______.
questions.question_raw: Separate xylem and phloem bundles are known as_______.
questions.explanation: Separate xylem and phloem bundles are found in dicotyledonous roots. This kind of vascular bundle is called the radial vascular bundle.

questions.question: The quiescent centre is found in the plant at:
questions.question_raw: The quiescent centre is found in the plant at:
questions.explanation: A quiescent centre is a small group of cells with low mitotic activity. It is found at the tips of growing roots in the root meristem.

questions.question: Ground tissue consists of:
questions.question_raw: Ground tissue consists of:

options.label: A
options.text: All tissues except epidermis and vascular tissue

correct_option_texts: All tissues except epidermis and vascular tissue
questions.explanation: The ground tissue of plants includes all tissues that are neither dermal nor vascular. It can be divided into three classes based on the nature of the cell walls.

questions.question: Lacunate collenchyma occurs in stem of______.
questions.question_raw: Lacunate collenchyma occurs in stem of______.
questions.explanation: Lacunate collenchyma is very much alike to angular collenchyma but the cells are regularly arranged. Thickening occurs at the inner and outer tangential wall. It is found in Cucurbita stem.

questions.question: In Barley stem, vascular bundles are:
questions.question_raw: In Barley stem, vascular bundles are:
questions.explanation: The vascular bundles in Hordeum vulgare (barley) plant are scattered in ground tissues, many in number, and vary in size-smaller towards the periphery and bigger towards the centre of the ground tissue, oval or rounded in outline, conjoint, collateral, and closed.

questions.question: Guard cells of stomata are thicker:
questions.question_raw: Guard cells of stomata are thicker:
questions.explanation: The cells surrounding the stomata are called guard cells. The guard cells of the inner side are thicker and that of outer side is comparatively thinner.

questions.question: The function of the guard cell in stomata is to:
questions.question_raw: The function of the guard cell in stomata is to:
questions.explanation: Opening and closing of stomatal pore of stomata is the function of guard cells. Change in osmotic potential cause the opening and closing of stomata.

questions.question: Upper surface of leaf is:
questions.question_raw: Upper surface of leaf is:
questions.explanation: Epidermal cells are present on both sides of the leaves. The lower side contains more stomata. The upper surface of the leaf is called the adaxial epidermis.

questions.question: Tyloses are balloon-like ingrowths in vessels developing from:
questions.question_raw: Tyloses are balloon-like ingrowths in vessels developing from:

options.label: A
options.text: Fibres through the general surface of the vessel wall

options.label: B
options.text: Parenchyma through the pits of vessels wall

options.label: C
options.text: Fibre through pits on vessels wall

options.label: D
options.text: Parenchyma through the general surface of the vessel wall

correct_option_texts: Parenchyma through the pits of vessels wall
questions.explanation: Tyloses are balloon-like ingrowths in vessels developing from parenchyma cells through the pits of the vessel wall of the xylem of secondary heartwood.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Anatomy of Flowering Plants

questions.question: Vascular bundles in which cambium is present between xylem and phloem is called as:
questions.question_raw: Vascular bundles in which cambium is present between xylem and phloem is called as:
questions.explanation: Vascular bundles in which cambium is present between xylem and phloem are called open vascular bundles and those in which cambium is absent are called closed vascular bundles.

questions.question: In angiosperms, the main water and mineral transporting elements are:
questions.question_raw: In angiosperms, the main water and mineral transporting elements are:
questions.explanation: Tracheids and Vessels

questions.question: Exarch xylem is present in______.
questions.question_raw: Exarch xylem is present in______.
questions.explanation: The xylem in which protoxylem is outside and metaxylem is inside is called exarch xylem. This kind of xylem is found in the roots of dicots.

questions.question: In arborescent monocots stem, a secondary cambium grows in:
questions.question_raw: In arborescent monocots stem, a secondary cambium grows in:
questions.explanation: In arborescent monocots stem, secondary cambium grows in the hypodermal region for secondary growth that increases the girth of the stem.

questions.question: Which type of vascular bundle is shown in the figure below
questions.question_raw: Which type of vascular bundle is shown in the figure below
questions.explanation: The vascular bundle shown below is closed and conjoint as cambium is absent between xylem and phloem tissue.

questions.question: In dicot stems, the cells of cambium present between the primary xylem and primary phloem are:
questions.question_raw: In dicot stems, the cells of cambium present between the primary xylem and primary phloem are:
questions.explanation: Intrafascicular Cambium is primary meristem. It develops from the procambium of the stem apex. It is located inside the open vascular bundles, between phloem and xylem patches.

questions.question: The secondary growth of woody angiosperm stem occurs by:
questions.question_raw: The secondary growth of woody angiosperm stem occurs by:
questions.explanation: The tissues involved in secondary growth are the two lateral meristems namely, vascular cambium and cork cambium.

questions.question: The wood is actually a:
questions.question_raw: The wood is actually a:
questions.explanation: Wood is a secondary xylem. It is secondary growth by cambium and constitutes the bulk of the plant body in dicot stems and dicot roots.

questions.question: Interfascicular cambium arise from the cells of:
questions.question_raw: Interfascicular cambium arise from the cells of:
questions.explanation:
During secondary growth, cells of medullary rays, in a line with intrafascicular cambium, become meristematic and form interfascicular cambium.
Therefore, the intrafascicular and interfascicular cambia form a ring which separates the primary xylem and primary phloem and is known as cambium ring.

questions.question: The region consisting of dead elements with highly lignified walls is called ________.
questions.question_raw: The region consisting of dead elements with highly lignified walls is called ________.
questions.explanation: In old trees, the greater part of secondary xylem is dark brown due to deposition of organic compounds like tannins, resins, oils, gums, aromatic substances and essential oils in the central or innermost layers of the stem. These substances make it hard, durable and resistant to the attacks of microorganisms and insects. This region comprises dead elements with highly lignified walls and is called heartwood.

questions.question: Cork cambium is also known as______.
questions.question_raw: Cork cambium is also known as______.
questions.explanation: As the stem continues to increase in girth due to the activity of vascular cambium, the outer cortical and epidermis layers get broken and need to be replaced to provide new protective cell layers. Hence, another meristematic tissue called cork cambium or phellogen develops in the cortex region.

questions.question: The cells of cambial ring cut off towards pith and mature into:
questions.question_raw: The cells of cambial ring cut off towards pith and mature into:
questions.explanation: The cambial ring becomes active and begins to cut off new cells, both towards the inner and the outer sides. The cells cut off towards pith, mature into secondary xylem.

questions.question: The phelloderm is also called _______.
questions.question_raw: The phelloderm is also called _______.
questions.explanation: The outer cells differentiate into cork or phellem while the inner cells differentiate into secondary cortex or phelloderm.

questions.question: Aerating pores in the bark of plants is known as _______.
questions.question_raw: Aerating pores in the bark of plants is known as _______.
questions.explanation: Lenticels permit the exchange of gases between the outer atmosphere and the internal tissue of the stem. These occur in most woody trees.

questions.question: Sclereids belongs to_______.
questions.question_raw: Sclereids belongs to_______.
questions.explanation: Sclereids belongs to sclerenchyma complex permanent tissue present in hard parts of plants. They are generally hard and founds in rough fruits and stem.

chapters.name: Structural Organisation in Animals
chapters.full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Structural Organisation in Animals

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Structural Organisation in Animals

questions.question: Where is ciliated epithelium found in the human body?
questions.question_raw: Where is ciliated epithelium found in the human body?
questions.explanation: The ciliated epithelium is mainly present in the inner surface of hollow organs like bronchioles and fallopian tubes.

questions.question: The epithelium found in the lining of the stomach and intestine is ________.
questions.question_raw: The epithelium found in the lining of the stomach and intestine is ________.
questions.explanation: The columnar epithelium is found in the lining of the stomach and intestine and help in secretion and absorption. It also provides a barrier against harmful microorganisms.

questions.question: The epithelium involved in diffusion is:
questions.question_raw: The epithelium involved in diffusion is:
questions.explanation: The squamous epithelium is made of a single thin layer of flattened cells with irregular boundaries. They are found in the walls of blood vessels and air sacs of the lungs and are involved in functions like forming a diffusion boundary.

questions.question: Collagen is:
questions.question_raw: Collagen is:
questions.explanation: In all connective tissues except blood, the cells secrete fibres of structural proteins called collagen or elastin.

questions.question: Fibres and fibroblasts are compactly packed in:
questions.question_raw: Fibres and fibroblasts are compactly packed in:
questions.explanation: Dense irregular connective tissue provides strength to body parts. Hence the cells and fibres are packed compactly.

questions.question: Tendons connect:
questions.question_raw: Tendons connect:
questions.explanation: Tendons are dense connective tissue which provides strength and attach muscles to bones.

questions.question: Which one of the following is not a connective tissue?
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following is not a connective tissue?
questions.explanation:
Blood, bone, lymph forms the connective tissue that connects the different parts of the body
Nerve cells are parts of neural system or nervous system that transmit information from one part of the body to others.

questions.question: The epithelium found in proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) of the nephron in the kidney is_______.
questions.question_raw: The epithelium found in proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) of the nephron in the kidney is_______.
questions.explanation: The cuboidal epithelium is composed of a single layer of cube-like cells found in ducts of glands and tubular parts of nephrons in kidneys and its main functions are secretion and absorption.

questions.question: What makes up more than one-half the volume of neural tissue in our body?
questions.question_raw: What makes up more than one-half the volume of neural tissue in our body?
questions.explanation: The neuroglial cells which constitute the rest of the neural system protect and support neurons. Neuroglia makes up more than one-half the volume of neural tissue in our body.

questions.question: Intercalated discs occur in:
questions.question_raw: Intercalated discs occur in:

options.label: B
options.text: At the junction of muscle and nerve cells

questions.explanation: In between cardiac muscle fibres, there are communication junctions which are made up of intercalated discs.

questions.question: Fibroblasts, macrophages, and mast cells are present in ______.
questions.question_raw: Fibroblasts, macrophages, and mast cells are present in ______.
questions.explanation: Apart from being connective tissue, Areolar tissue is also protective in nature. It has a large number of fibroblast cells that produce fibres. Mast cells, plasma cells, and macrophages help to protect against pathogens and aid in tissue repair.

questions.question: The fibre that can't be made to contract merely by thinking about it, is the:
questions.question_raw: The fibre that can't be made to contract merely by thinking about it, is the:
questions.explanation: Smooth muscles are ‘involuntary’ muscles as they can't be directly controlled. We usually are not able to make it contract merely by thinking about it as we can do with skeletal muscles. Involuntary muscles are under the control of medulla oblongata and part of the cerebrum. Smooth muscles are spindle-shaped or fusiform, uninucleated, and unstriated fibres. The wall of internal organs such as the blood vessels, stomach, and intestine contains this type of muscle tissue.

questions.question: Identify the figure given below:
questions.question_raw: Identify the figure given below:
questions.explanation: The figure is identified as salivary gland. It is multicellular and is lined with glandular epithelium cells. These types of cells are specialized in production and release of particular kind of digestive juices or sweat or oil. Salivary gland secretes alkaline fluid called saliva.

questions.question: To which one of the following categories does adipose tissue belong?
questions.question_raw: To which one of the following categories does adipose tissue belong?
questions.explanation: Adipose tissue is a type of connective tissue consisting mainly of adipose cells or adipocytes, specialized to synthesize and contain large globules of fat.

questions.question: The junctions which help to stop substances from leaking across a tissue is:
questions.question_raw: The junctions which help to stop substances from leaking across a tissue is:
questions.explanation: Tight junctions hold cells together. They are narrow belts that circle around the upper part of lateral surfaces of the adjacent epithelial cells to create a fusion point. They stop substances from leaking across a tissue as they prevent the passage of molecules and ions through space between adjacent cells.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Structural Organisation in Animals

questions.question: The most abundant type of animal tissue in the complex animals is:
questions.question_raw: The most abundant type of animal tissue in the complex animals is:
questions.explanation: The most abundant type of animal tissue connective tissue as it forms a variety of tissues such as blood, bone, cartilage, adipose, lymph, tendon, and ligament. These connective tissue types perform a wide range of functions in the animal body such as giving support, packing, repairing, storing of fat, connecting different organs, etc.

questions.question: The formation of erythrocytes in foetus takes place in:
questions.question_raw: The formation of erythrocytes in foetus takes place in:
questions.explanation: In the early fetus, erythropoiesis takes place in the mesodermal cells of the yolk sac. Later, the production of RBCs starts in the fetal liver and increases until the 2nd^ trimester. After this, the rate of synthesis begins to decline in the liver as erythropoiesis increases in the bone marrow. RBCs are still produced in the liver for approximately 1 week after birth.

questions.question: Study of tissues is called:
questions.question_raw: Study of tissues is called:
questions.explanation: Study of organisms at tissues level is called histology. It includes the study of tissue structure, origin, location and function.

questions.question: The epithelium covering in the tongue is:
questions.question_raw: The epithelium covering in the tongue is:
questions.explanation: The tongue is covered by non-keratinized squamous epithelial tissues having taste buds of different kinds in different regions of the tongue.

questions.question: Two animal cells are interconnected by:
questions.question_raw: Two animal cells are interconnected by:
questions.explanation: Two animal cells are interconnected by desmosomes, through which the exchange of materials takes place between two cells. It is formed from protein plaques in the cell membranes linked by filaments.

questions.question: Myelin sheath is derived from:
questions.question_raw: Myelin sheath is derived from:
questions.explanation: The outer layer of the axon of nerve cells is called the myelin sheath. Myelin sheath is derived from Schwann cells.

questions.question: Antibodies are formed by:
questions.question_raw: Antibodies are formed by:
questions.explanation: Antibodies are formed against any foreign body entering the body. It is formed by plasma cells, globulins that develop resistance against a particular type of disease.

questions.question: The cell lining the blood vessels are:
questions.question_raw: The cell lining the blood vessels are:
questions.explanation: The endothelium is a type of epithelium that lines the interior surface of blood vessels and lymphatic vessels, forming an interface between circulating blood or lymph in the lumen and the rest of the vessel wall. It is a thin layer of simple squamous called endothelial cells.

questions.question: Cardiac muscles are:
questions.question_raw: Cardiac muscles are:
questions.explanation: Cardiac muscle tissue is a contractile tissue present only in the heart. The cardiac muscle fibres are elongated, branched and uninucleate. They appear striated due to the presence of alternate light and dark band. Cardiac muscles are self-contracting and involuntary in nature. The contract in a rhythmic fashion and help the heart to pump the blood.

questions.question: Which of the following structure is Ectodermal in origin?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following structure is Ectodermal in origin?
questions.explanation: The ectoderm differentiates to form the nervous system (spine, peripheral nerves and brain), tooth enamel and the epidermis, the outer part of the integument. It also forms the lining of mouth, anus, nostrils, sweat glands, hair and nails.

questions.question: A normal adult man and woman have ________ WBC per cubic millimetre.
questions.question_raw: A normal adult man and woman have ________ WBC per cubic millimetre.
questions.explanation: A normal adult male and female have about 4.5 to 5 million of WBC per cubic millimetre of blood. The number of white blood cells increases during infection against pathogens.

questions.question: The most active phagocytic white blood cells are:
questions.question_raw: The most active phagocytic white blood cells are:
questions.explanation: The most active phagocytic white blood cells are neutrophils and monocytes. These cells try to engulf the microbes that enter the blood to cause disease.

questions.question: Nasal septum contains:
questions.question_raw: Nasal septum contains:
questions.explanation: The nasal septum of human beings consists of hyaline cartilage which is highly elastic having collagen. It contains no nerves or blood vessels and is pearly bluish in colour.

questions.question: Lymph contains:
questions.question_raw: Lymph contains:
questions.explanation: Lymph contains serum and WBC. Lymph is called extracellular fluid that helps in the transport of fat and protein. It contains WBC, so also help in protection against the infection.

questions.question: Intercalated discs occur:
questions.question_raw: Intercalated discs occur:

options.label: B
options.text: At the junction of muscle and nerve cells

questions.explanation: Intercalated discs are microscopic identifying features of cardiac muscles fibres. Individual heart muscle cells are connected by intercalated discs to work as a single functional organ.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Structural Organisation in Animals

questions.question: Basophils help the body defence by:
questions.question_raw: Basophils help the body defence by:
questions.explanation: Basophils appear in many specific kinds of inflammatory reactions, particularly those that cause allergic symptoms. Basophils contain anticoagulant heparin, which prevents blood from clotting too quickly. (Thrombosisis the formation of a blood clot inside a blood vessel, obstructing the flow of blood through the circulatory system.) They also contain the vasodilator histamine, which promotes blood flow to tissues.

questions.question: The life span of RBC and WBC are respectively:
questions.question_raw: The life span of RBC and WBC are respectively:
questions.explanation: Red blood cells (RBC) live for 120 days and white blood cells (WBC) live for 90 days. Afterwards, they are degraded in the spleen.

questions.question: Nissl granules in a nerve cell are now identified as_______.
questions.question_raw: Nissl granules in a nerve cell are now identified as_______.
questions.explanation: The Nissl granules in nerve cells are now identified as ribosomes after the discovery of an electron microscope that provides a highly magnified image for detailed study.

questions.question: Which one of the following is the correct pairing of a body part and muscle fibre that moves it?
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following is the correct pairing of a body part and muscle fibre that moves it?

options.label: B
options.text: Biceps of upper arm - smooth muscle fibres

questions.explanation: The iris contains involuntary smooth muscles. It encircles the pupil of the iris and helps to dilate the pupil when there is insufficient light for the normal function of the eye.

questions.question: Which of the following leucocytes transforms into macrophages?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following leucocytes transforms into macrophages?
questions.explanation: Monocytes are white blood cells or leucocytes that transform into macrophages to engulf the pathogen entering the body.

questions.question: Intestinal mucosa has microvili to:
questions.question_raw: Intestinal mucosa has microvili to:
questions.explanation: The innermost layer of the intestinal wall is called the mucosa. Mucosa contains microvilli to increase the surface area for the absorption of digested food to transport into a different part of the body.

questions.question: Which one is a heterocrine gland?
questions.question_raw: Which one is a heterocrine gland?
questions.explanation: The pancreas is called heterocrine gland because it acts as both endocrine and exocrine gland to secrete hormone as well as digestive enzymes. Testis and Ovary are also heterocrine glands.

questions.question: Blood plasma is pale yellow in colour due to:
questions.question_raw: Blood plasma is pale yellow in colour due to:
questions.explanation: Blood plasma is pale yellow in colour due to the presence of bilirubin pigment in it. Blood appears red due to the presence of haemoglobin in RBC.

questions.question: Muscle forming cells are called:
questions.question_raw: Muscle forming cells are called:
questions.explanation: The muscle forming cells are called myoblasts cells. Myoblast is a type of embryonic progenitor cell that differentiates to give rise to muscle cells.

questions.question: Which layer produces a new cell by mitosis?
questions.question_raw: Which layer produces a new cell by mitosis?
questions.explanation: Stratum Malpighi is the basal layer of skin epidermis having basal layer and generative layer that produces new cells by mitosis.

questions.question: A largest single mass of lymphatic tissue in the body is______.
questions.question_raw: A largest single mass of lymphatic tissue in the body is______.
questions.explanation: Spleen is the largest single mass of lymphatic tissue in the body. Blood formation in the foetus and newly born child takes place in spleen.

questions.question: Both WBC and RBC are formed in the:
questions.question_raw: Both WBC and RBC are formed in the:
questions.explanation: In adults, both WBC and RBC are formed in the bone marrow that is located in long bones such as thighs.

questions.question: Mature erythrocytes cannot utilise glucose because they lack_______.
questions.question_raw: Mature erythrocytes cannot utilise glucose because they lack_______.
questions.explanation: Mature erythrocytes or red blood corpuscles cannot utilize glucose because these cells lack mitochondria as well as the nucleus. Though glycolysis or glucose breakdown occurs in the cytoplasm, the final breakdown of pyruvate takes place inside mitochondria. Also, the electron transport chain is located inside mitochondria. Hence, the presence of mitochondria is essential for a cell to utilize glucose and release energy.

questions.question: The matrix of bone and cartilage can be distinguished by the presence of ________.
questions.question_raw: The matrix of bone and cartilage can be distinguished by the presence of ________.
questions.explanation: The matrix of bone and cartilage differ by the presence of a Haversian canal that makes bone hard and stiff. On the other hand, cartilage is flexible.

questions.question: The ciliated columnar epithelium cells in human are known to occur in:
questions.question_raw: The ciliated columnar epithelium cells in human are known to occur in:
questions.explanation: Ciliated columnar epithelium cells in humans occur in bronchioles and fallopian tube. They contain small hair-like cilia on the free surface. This helps to move egg into the fallopian tube. In bronchioles, it helps to move mucus or dust particles.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Structural Organisation in Animals

questions.question: Whales can live in cold water as they have thick coat of_________.
questions.question_raw: Whales can live in cold water as they have thick coat of_________.
questions.explanation: Whales can live in cold water as they have a thick coat of blubber. Blubber help in providing insulating coat beneath the skin that prevents loss of heat from the body.

questions.question: Nerve fibres in the CNS can regenerate after injury due to the presence of________.
questions.question_raw: Nerve fibres in the CNS can regenerate after injury due to the presence of________.
questions.explanation: Nerve fibres in the CNS can regenerate after injury due to the presence of neurilemma. They are the outermost nucleated cytoplasmic layer of Schwann cells that surrounds the axon of the neuron.

questions.question: Platelets are formed from the________.
questions.question_raw: Platelets are formed from the________.
questions.explanation: Platelets are a little piece of blood cells that help wound healing and prevent bleeding by forming a blood clot. It is formed from the megakaryocytes.

questions.question: Certain neutrophils in female mammals possess a small spherical lobe attached to their nucleus by a stalk. This lobe is called ________.
questions.question_raw: Certain neutrophils in female mammals possess a small spherical lobe attached to their nucleus by a stalk. This lobe is called ________.
questions.explanation: A Barr body appears as a small drumstick-like projection on one of the lobes of some neutrophils in females. A Barr body is the inactive X chromosome in a female somatic cell.

questions.question: The type of connective tissue that is associated with the umbilical cord is:
questions.question_raw: The type of connective tissue that is associated with the umbilical cord is:
questions.explanation: Jelly like connective tissue is associated with the umbilical cord. It is a flexible cord-like structure containing blood vessels and attaching a human fetus to the placenta during gestation.

questions.question: The haemoglobin content per 100 ml of blood of a normal healthy human adult is:
questions.question_raw: The haemoglobin content per 100 ml of blood of a normal healthy human adult is:
questions.explanation: The haemoglobin content per 100 ml of blood of a normal healthy human adult is in the range of 12-16 g. Haemoglobin acts as a respiratory carrier. It is responsible for picking up oxygen from the lungs and transporting it to all parts of the body through blood. Maintaining a healthy level of haemoglobin in blood is essential for sufficient supply of oxygen to cells or tissues. Level of haemoglobin less than 12g/100ml leads to anaemia and weakness.

questions.question: The type of muscles present in our:
questions.question_raw: The type of muscles present in our:

options.label: A
options.text: the heart is involuntary and unstriated smooth muscle.

options.label: B
options.text: the upper arm is smooth muscle and fusiform in shape.

options.label: C
options.text: the intestine is striated and involuntary.

options.label: D
options.text: the thigh is striated and voluntary.

correct_option_texts: the thigh is striated and voluntary.
questions.explanation: The muscle present in our thigh is striated and voluntary muscles. They are attached to the bone for movement and works according to our will.

questions.question: Choose the odd pair out in the following:
questions.question_raw: Choose the odd pair out in the following:
questions.explanation: Epithelium tissues contain keratin, muscles fibres contain actin and areolar connective tissue contain collagen but melanin is absent in neuron.

questions.question: A close approximation of an axon of one neuron and a dendrite of the next is called ________.
questions.question_raw: A close approximation of an axon of one neuron and a dendrite of the next is called ________.
questions.explanation: The close connection between an axon and dendrite of the next neuron is called a synapse. Nerve impulses pass through it in the form of neurotransmitters due to the presence of synaptic fluid present in it.

questions.question: The muscle which is immune to fatigue are:
questions.question_raw: The muscle which is immune to fatigue are:
questions.explanation: The muscles which are immune to fatigue are cardiac muscles present exclusively in the heart. They work from birth till death without getting fatigued.

questions.question: The mammary gland is an example of:
questions.question_raw: The mammary gland is an example of:
questions.explanation: Apocrine glands are a kind of exocrine gland found in the skin, breast, eyelids, and ears. Mammary glands are present in the breast that release milk in mammals.

questions.question: In earthworm, two pairs of testes are present in:
questions.question_raw: In earthworm, two pairs of testes are present in:
questions.explanation: There are two pairs of testes present in the 10th and 11th segments. Their vasa deferentia run-up to the 18th segment where they join the prostatic duct.

questions.question: How many types of nephridia are seen in earthworm based on their location?
questions.question_raw: How many types of nephridia are seen in earthworm based on their location?
questions.explanation:
The excretory organs occur as segmentally arranged coiled tubules called nephridia.
They are of three types:
 septal nephridia
 integumentary nephridia
 pharyngeal nephridia.

questions.question: The pharyngeal nephridia of earthworm occur in segments:
questions.question_raw: The pharyngeal nephridia of earthworm occur in segments:
questions.explanation: The excretory system of earthworm contains a pair of nephridia in every segment, except for the first three and the last ones. Three types of nephridia are found in earthworm: integumentary, septal, and pharyngeal. The pharyngeal nephridia are present as three paired tufts in the 4th, 5th, and 6th segments.

questions.question: Earthworms are known as Friends of farmers because:
questions.question_raw: Earthworms are known as Friends of farmers because:

options.label: B
options.text: They are used as meal for farmers

questions.explanation: Earthworms are known as ‘friends of farmers’ because they make burrows in the soil and make it porous which helps in respiration and penetration of the developing plant roots.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Structural Organisation in Animals

questions.question: In Pheretima, ovaries are present in:
questions.question_raw: In Pheretima, ovaries are present in:
questions.explanation: In Pheretima a pair of whitish, small, lobulated ovaries are present on either side of the ventral nerve cord in 13th segments.

questions.question: In earthworm, the stomach is present in:
questions.question_raw: In earthworm, the stomach is present in:
questions.explanation: The stomach of an earthworm is a long cylindrical structure and extends from 9-14 segments.

questions.question:
Which statement is wrong with respect to an earthworm?
 Muscular layer in the body wall of an earthworm is made up of circular muscles.
 Setae help in the locomotion.
 A single male genital pore is present.
 Typhlosole is the part of the intestine.
questions.question_raw:
Which statement is wrong with respect to an earthworm?

  1. Muscular layer in the body wall of an earthworm is made up of circular muscles.
  2. Setae help in the locomotion.
  3. A single male genital pore is present.
  4. Typhlosole is the part of the intestine.
questions.explanation: The body wall of the earthworm is covered externally by a thin non-cellular cuticle below which is the epidermis, two muscle layers namely, circular and longitudinal, and an innermost coelomic epithelium.

questions.question: The clitellum in earthworm occur in:
questions.question_raw: The clitellum in earthworm occur in:
questions.explanation: In a mature worm, segments 14-16 are covered by a prominent dark band of glandular tissue called the clitellum.

questions.question: Which of the following structure is Ectodermal in origin in the mouthparts of cockroach shown in the figure below?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following structure is Ectodermal in origin in the mouthparts of cockroach shown in the figure below?
questions.explanation: The maxilla is a paired structure present in the mouth part of cockroaches. It is derived from ectoderm and is used to hold and manipulate food.

questions.question: How many pairs of spiracles are found in cockroach?
questions.question_raw: How many pairs of spiracles are found in cockroach?
questions.explanation: The respiratory system consists of a network of the trachea. This network opens through 10 pairs of small holes called spiracles present on the lateral side of the body.

questions.question: Identify ‘A’ in the figure given below.
questions.question_raw: Identify ‘A’ in the figure given below.
questions.explanation: The label ‘A’ is ocellus. It is a type of photoreceptor found in some animals like cockroaches.

questions.question: In male cockroaches, genital pouch lies at the hind end of the abdomen bounded by:
questions.question_raw: In male cockroaches, genital pouch lies at the hind end of the abdomen bounded by:

options.label: A
options.text: Dorsally by 9th and 10th terga and ventrally by the 9th sternum

options.label: B
options.text: Dorsally by 8th and 9th terga and ventrally by the 9th sternum

options.label: C
options.text: Dorsally by 8th and 9th terga and ventrally by the 8th sternum

options.label: D
options.text: Dorsally by 9th and 10th terga and ventrally by the 10th sternum

correct_option_texts: Dorsally by 9th and 10th terga and ventrally by the 9th sternum
questions.explanation: In males, genital pouch or chamber lies at the hind end of abdomen bounded dorsally by 9th and 10th terga and ventrally by the 9th sternum. It contains dorsal anus, ventral male genital pore, and gonapophysis.

questions.question: Which one of the following statements is true for cockroach?
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following statements is true for cockroach?

options.label: A
options.text: The larval stage is called caterpillar

options.label: B
options.text: The number of ovarioles in each ovary are ten

options.label: D
options.text: Anal styles are absent in females

correct_option_texts: Anal styles are absent in females
questions.explanation: Male cockroaches bear a pair of short, thread-like anal styles in the 9th segment which is absent in females.

questions.question: The number of eggs contained in an ootheca of cockroach is:
questions.question_raw: The number of eggs contained in an ootheca of cockroach is:
questions.explanation: On average, females produce 9-10 oothecae. Each ootheca contains 14-16 eggs.

questions.question: The number of ganglia in the nervous system of cockroach is:
questions.question_raw: The number of ganglia in the nervous system of cockroach is:

options.label: A
options.text: 6 in thorax and 3 in the abdomen

options.label: B
options.text: 4 in thorax and 6 in the abdomen

options.label: C
options.text: 3 in thorax and 6 in the abdomen

options.label: D
options.text: 4 in thorax and 3 in the abdomen

correct_option_texts: 3 in thorax and 6 in the abdomen
questions.explanation: The nervous system of cockroach consists of a series of fused, segmentally arranged ganglia. They are joined by paired longitudinal connectives on the ventral side. Three ganglia lie in the thorax, and six ganglia are found in the abdomen.

questions.question: What type of metamorphosis occurs in cockroach?
questions.question_raw: What type of metamorphosis occurs in cockroach?
questions.explanation: The development of cockroach is paurometabolous. This means a gradual development from the nymphal stage to the adult stage. Nymph represents a miniature adult and lives in the same habitat.

questions.question: In cockroach, a mushroom-shaped gland is present in:
questions.question_raw: In cockroach, a mushroom-shaped gland is present in:
questions.explanation: A characteristic mushroom-shaped gland is present in the 6th-7th abdominal segments of the cockroach. This gland functions as an accessory reproductive gland.

questions.question:
Match the following and choose the correct answer with reference to cockroach:
 Column A
 Column B
 (i) Bundles of sperm
 (a) Ovarioles
 (ii) External genitalia
 (b) Gonopore
 (iii) Chain of developing ova
 (c) Phallomere
 (iv) Opening of ejaculatory duct
 (d) Spermatophore
questions.question_raw:
Match the following and choose the correct answer with reference to cockroach:

Column A Column B
(i) Bundles of sperm (a) Ovarioles
(ii) External genitalia (b) Gonopore
(iii) Chain of developing ova (c) Phallomere
(iv) Opening of ejaculatory duct (d) Spermatophore
questions.explanation:
The sperms are stored in the seminal vesicles and are glued together in the form of bundles called spermatophores which are discharged during copulation.
 The external genitalia is represented by chitinous asymmetrical structures surrounding the male gonopore is called male gonapophysis or phallomere.
 Each ovary is formed of a group of eight ovarian tubules or ovarioles, containing a chain of developing ova. 
 The ejaculatory duct opens into a male gonopore situated ventral to the anus.

questions.question: The pericardial space in cockroach is regularly altered by the muscles:
questions.question_raw: The pericardial space in cockroach is regularly altered by the muscles:
questions.explanation: Cockroaches have an open circulatory system. Their pericardial space is regularly altered by alary muscles.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Structural Organisation in Animals

questions.question: The mouth part of cockroach which is compared to the tongue is_______.
questions.question_raw: The mouth part of cockroach which is compared to the tongue is_______.
questions.explanation: A median flexible lobe called a hypopharynx act as the tongue. It lies within the cavity enclosed by the mouthparts.

questions.question: How many times do nymphs moult to reach the adult form of a cockroach?
questions.question_raw: How many times do nymphs moult to reach the adult form of a cockroach?
questions.explanation: When the ootheca hatches, a tiny nymph comes out. It grows by undergoing molting about 13 times to reach the adult form.

questions.question: Blood consists of:
questions.question_raw: Blood consists of:
questions.explanation: Blood is a fluid connective tissue containing plasma. It contains red blood cells (RBC), white blood cells (WBC), and platelets suspended in the plasma.

questions.question: In frog, during aestivation and hibernation gaseous exchange takes place through
questions.question_raw: In frog, during aestivation and hibernation gaseous exchange takes place through
questions.explanation: As the frog becomes inactive during aestivation and hibernation, gaseous exchange does not take place through lungs. It takes place through skin.

questions.question: The lymph in frog lacks:
questions.question_raw: The lymph in frog lacks:
questions.explanation: The lymph is different from the blood. It is known as tissue fluid. Lymph is yellowish in colour as it lacks RBCs and few proteins.

questions.question: The winter sleep in frog is called ________.
questions.question_raw: The winter sleep in frog is called ________.
questions.explanation: During extreme heat and cold periods, the frog takes shelter in deep burrows to protect them from extreme environmental conditions. This is called as summer sleep (aestivation) and winter sleep (hibernation).

questions.question:
The correct order of the digestive system of the frog is:
 Mouth, buccal cavity, oesophagus, stomach, intestine, rectum, cloaca, cloacal aperture
 Mouth, oesophagus, buccal cavity, stomach, intestine, rectum, cloacal aperture, cloaca
 Mouth, buccal cavity, oesophagus, intestine, stomach, rectum, cloacal aperture, cloaca
 Mouth, oesophagus, buccal cavity, stomach, rectum, intestine, cloaca, cloacal aperture, cloaca
questions.question_raw:
The correct order of the digestive system of the frog is:

  1. Mouth, buccal cavity, oesophagus, stomach, intestine, rectum, cloaca, cloacal aperture
  2. Mouth, oesophagus, buccal cavity, stomach, intestine, rectum, cloacal aperture, cloaca
  3. Mouth, buccal cavity, oesophagus, intestine, stomach, rectum, cloacal aperture, cloaca
  4. Mouth, oesophagus, buccal cavity, stomach, rectum, intestine, cloaca, cloacal aperture, cloaca
questions.explanation: The digestive system of a frog begins from the mouth which opens into the buccal cavity that leads to the esophagus through the pharynx. The esophagus is a short tube that opens into the stomach which in turn continues as the intestine and then rectum. The rectum finally opens outside by the cloaca through the cloacal aperture.

questions.question: The special venous connection between liver and intestine as well as the kidney and lower parts of the body present in frogs is called ________.
questions.question_raw: The special venous connection between liver and intestine as well as the kidney and lower parts of the body present in frogs is called ________.
questions.explanation: The renal portal system forms a special blood circulation system between liver and intestine. It also forms a special connection between the kidney and lower parts of the body present in frogs.

questions.question: The number of nymphs produced from single ootheca of the frog is _______.
questions.question_raw: The number of nymphs produced from single ootheca of the frog is _______.
questions.explanation: Young ones of frogs are called nymphs. When the ootheca ruptures, 16 nymphs come out.

questions.question:
Match the following with reference to the frog:
 Column A
 Column B
 (i) Medulla oblongata
 (a) Sensory papillae
 (ii) Smell
 (b) PNS
 (iii) Touch
 (c) Foramen magnum
 (iv) Cranial nerves
 (d) Nasal epithelium
questions.question_raw:
Match the following with reference to the frog:

Column A Column B
(i) Medulla oblongata (a) Sensory papillae
(ii) Smell (b) PNS
(iii) Touch (c) Foramen magnum
(iv) Cranial nerves (d) Nasal epithelium
questions.explanation:
The medulla oblongata passes out through the foramen magnum and continues into the spinal cord, which is enclosed in the vertebral column.
 Frog has different types of sense organs. This includes organs of touch (sensory papillae) and smell (nasal epithelium).
 The nervous system is organized into a central nervous system including the brain and spinal cord and peripheral nervous system (PNS) including cranial and spinal nerves.

questions.question: Blood plasma contains ________ % water.
questions.question_raw: Blood plasma contains ________ % water.
questions.explanation: Blood consists of blood plasma formed cells ( RBC, WBC, Platelets) along with sugar, proteins, etc. Blood plasma contains about 90% water with a number of salts and few circulating hormones.

questions.question: Myelin sheath is mainly made of______.
questions.question_raw: Myelin sheath is mainly made of______.
questions.explanation: The myelin sheath is the outer layer of the axon of nerve cells. It is made up of phospholipids. It is mainly protective in nature, acts as an insulator, and helps in the transmission of impulses.

questions.question: The formation of erythrocytes in foetus takes place in:
questions.question_raw: The formation of erythrocytes in foetus takes place in:
questions.explanation: In the early fetus, erythropoiesis takes place in the mesodermal cells of the yolk sac. Later, the production of RBCs starts in the fetal liver and increases until the 2nd^ trimester. After this, the rate of synthesis begins to decline in the liver as erythropoiesis increases in the bone marrow. RBCs are still produced in the liver for approximately 1 week after birth.

questions.question: Nerve fibres in the CNS can regenerate after injury due to the presence of________.
questions.question_raw: Nerve fibres in the CNS can regenerate after injury due to the presence of________.
questions.explanation: Nerve fibres in the CNS can regenerate after injury due to the presence of neurilemma. They are the outermost nucleated cytoplasmic layer of Schwann cells that surrounds the axon of the neuron.

questions.question: The most active phagocytic white blood cells are:
questions.question_raw: The most active phagocytic white blood cells are:
questions.explanation: The most active phagocytic white blood cells are neutrophils and monocytes. These cells try to engulf the microbes that enter the blood to cause disease.

chapters.name: Cell The Unit of Life
chapters.full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Cell The Unit of Life

tests.testPaperName: Cell The Unit Of Life Test-01
tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Cell The Unit of Life

questions.question: New cells generate from:
questions.question_raw: New cells generate from:
questions.explanation: All living organisms are composed of cells and products of cells.all cells arise from pre-existing cells.

questions.question: Who first saw and described a live cell?
questions.question_raw: Who first saw and described a live cell?
questions.explanation: The first man to witness a live cell under a microscope was Anton van Leeuwenhoek, who in 1674 described the algae Spirogyra. Van Leeuwenhoek probably also saw bacteria.

questions.question: Cytoskeleton is made of.
questions.question_raw: Cytoskeleton is made of.
questions.explanation: Cytoskeleton is a series of intercellular proteins that help a cell with shape, support andmovement. So, cytoskeleton is made up of proteinaceous filaments.

questions.question: Select the incorrect pairing:
questions.question_raw: Select the incorrect pairing:
questions.explanation: Mitochondria contain cristae, finger-like projection of inner layer, thylakoid is present in chloroplast and lysosome is a digestive enzyme but RNA is absent in Chromosome.

questions.question: Which is common in plant and animal cells?
questions.question_raw: Which is common in plant and animal cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are present in both plants as well as animal cells. Plastids and central vacuoles are present in plant cells and centrioles are present in only animal cells.

questions.question: Animal cell does not possess:
questions.question_raw: Animal cell does not possess:
questions.explanation: An animal cell does not possess plasmodesmata but they contain ribosomes, centriole, and plasmalemma. Plasmodesmata are the connection between two plant cells.

questions.question: The ability of a living somatic nucleated cell to form the complete organism is called:
questions.question_raw: The ability of a living somatic nucleated cell to form the complete organism is called:
questions.explanation: Cellular Totipotency is the ability of a single cell to produce all cell types and to organize them into an entire organism when cultured in a suitable culture medium at the appropriate temperature and aeration conditions. Spores and Zygote are examples of totipotent cells.

questions.question: The most recent and accepted model of the plasma membrane is:
questions.question_raw: The most recent and accepted model of the plasma membrane is:
questions.explanation: Fluid-Mosaic model is the most recent and accepted model of the plasma membrane. This model was proposed by Singer and Nicolson in 1972. The quasi-fluid nature of lipid enables the lateral movement of proteins within the overall bilayer.

questions.question: Each centriole is surrounded by dense amorphous protoplasmic masses called:
questions.question_raw: Each centriole is surrounded by dense amorphous protoplasmic masses called:
questions.explanation: All animal cell has two small organelles called centrioles. Each centriole is surrounded by dense amorphous protoplasmic masses called Massules.

questions.question: The photograph or diagram of metaphase chromosome arranged in homologous pair according to their length and thickness, the position of the centromere is called ________.
questions.question_raw: The photograph or diagram of metaphase chromosome arranged in homologous pair according to their length and thickness, the position of the centromere is called ________.
questions.explanation: Idiogram is a diagrammatic or systematized representation of the chromosome complement of an individual, species, or another group of organisms, indicating the number of chromosomes and their lengths, arm length ratio, centromere position, banding pattern.

questions.question: The membrane complex formed in the folding of the plasma membrane in prokaryotic cells is called ________.
questions.question_raw: The membrane complex formed in the folding of the plasma membrane in prokaryotic cells is called ________.
questions.explanation: A special membranous structure is a mesosome which is formed by the extensions of the plasma membrane into the cell.

questions.question: The smallest free-living organism is______.
questions.question_raw: The smallest free-living organism is______.
questions.explanation: PPLO (Pleuro Pneumonia Like Organisms). They are generally smaller and multiply more rapidly than the eukaryotic cells.

questions.question: In certain bacteria, the glycocalyx is formed into loose sheath layer is called as ________.
questions.question_raw: In certain bacteria, the glycocalyx is formed into loose sheath layer is called as ________.
questions.explanation: Glycocalyx differs in composition and thickness among different bacteria. It could be a loose sheath called the slime layer in some, while in others it may be thick and tough called the capsule. The cell wall determines the shape of the cell and provides strong structural support.

questions.question: The bacterial flagellum consists of
questions.question_raw: The bacterial flagellum consists of
questions.explanation:
A bacterial flagellum has 3 basic parts: a filament, a hook, and a basal body.
The filament is the rigid, helical structure that extends from the cell surface.
The hook is a flexible coupling between the filament and the basal body.
The basal body consists of a rod and a series of rings that anchor the flagellum to the cell wall and the cytoplasmic membrane.

questions.question: The rod-shaped bacteria is called as_______.
questions.question_raw: The rod-shaped bacteria is called as_______.
questions.explanation: The shapes of bacteria are bacillus (rod-like), coccus (spherical), vibrio (comma-shaped) and spirillum (spiral).

tests.testPaperName: Cell The Unit Of Life Test-02
tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Cell The Unit of Life

questions.question: The rod-shaped bacteria is called as_______.
questions.question_raw: The rod-shaped bacteria is called as_______.
questions.explanation: The shapes of bacteria are bacillus (rod-like), coccus (spherical), vibrio (comma-shaped) and spirillum (spiral).

questions.question: In prokaryotes, the reserved food material is stored in:
questions.question_raw: In prokaryotes, the reserved food material is stored in:
questions.explanation: Reserve material in prokaryotic cells is stored in the cytoplasm in the form of inclusion bodies.

questions.question: Tubulin occurs in:
questions.question_raw: Tubulin occurs in:
questions.explanation: Tubulin is a kind of protein that is present in the cilia and flagella of cells having it. It provides flexibility to the cilia and flagella.

questions.question: Arrangement of ciliary microtubules is:
questions.question_raw: Arrangement of ciliary microtubules is:
questions.explanation: Cilia present in prokaryotic organisms consist of microtubules. These microtubules are arranged in 9+2 order in which 2 forms the centre and 9 are arranged at the periphery.

questions.question: Which does not occur in cell membrane?
questions.question_raw: Which does not occur in cell membrane?
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is made up of lipid bilayer consisting of different components like phospholipids, glycolipids, and cholesterol. However, proline is an alpha-amino acid used in protein synthesis and is a major constituent of collagen.

questions.question: Lipid molecules of plasma membrane occur
questions.question_raw: Lipid molecules of plasma membrane occur
questions.explanation: Phospholipids found in the plasma membrane is a lipid bilayer having a hydrophilic region called the head and a hydrophobic region called the tail. The hydrophilic region can interact with water due to the presence of a phosphate group which is polar, like water. The membrane is in contact with water on both the inner and outer sides. The hydrophilic heads arrange automatically on the outer side of the bilayer due to polarity and hydrophobic heads are facing each other between the bilayer, thus arranging all lipid molecules parallel to each other.

questions.question: The plasma membrane consists mainly of:
questions.question_raw: The plasma membrane consists mainly of:

options.label: A
options.text: Proteins embedded in a carbohydrate bilayer

options.label: B
options.text: Proteins embedded in a phospholipids bilayer

options.label: D
options.text: Proteins embedded in a polymer of glucose molecules

correct_option_texts: Proteins embedded in a phospholipids bilayer
questions.explanation: The plasma membrane is composed of lipids that are arranged in a bilayer. Also, the lipids are arranged within the membrane with the polar head towards the outer sides and the hydrophobic tails towards the inner part. The membrane proteins can be classified as integral or peripheral. Peripheral proteins lie on the surface.

questions.question: The nucleus was first described by:
questions.question_raw: The nucleus was first described by:
questions.explanation: Nucleus as a cell organelle was first described by Robert Brown as early as 1831.

questions.question: Lysosomes are produced by:
questions.question_raw: Lysosomes are produced by:
questions.explanation: Lysosomes are manufactured and budded into the cytoplasm by the Golgi apparatus with enzymes inside. The enzymes that are within the lysosome are made in the rough endoplasmic reticulum, which is then delivered to the Golgi apparatus via transport vesicles.

questions.question: The eukaryotic ribosomes are of_____.
questions.question_raw: The eukaryotic ribosomes are of_____.
questions.explanation: The eukaryotic ribosomes are the 80S while the prokaryotic ribosomes are 70S. Here ‘S’ stands for the sedimentation coefficient; it indirectly is a measure of density and size.

questions.question: The vacuoles are bound by the single membrane is called as_________.
questions.question_raw: The vacuoles are bound by the single membrane is called as_________.
questions.explanation: The vacuole is bound by a single membrane called tonoplast. In-plant cells the vacuoles can occupy up to 90% of the volume of the cell. In plants, the tonoplast facilitates the transport of a number of ions.

questions.question: The chromosome having centromeres at one terminal end is called ________.
questions.question_raw: The chromosome having centromeres at one terminal end is called ________.
questions.explanation: A telocentric chromosome's centromere is located at the terminal end of the chromosome. Telomeres may extend from both ends of the chromosome their shape is like I during anaphase. For example, the standard house mouse karyotype has only telocentric chromosomes. Humans do not possess telocentric chromosomes.

questions.question: Nucleoplasm otherwise called as ______.
questions.question_raw: Nucleoplasm otherwise called as ______.
questions.explanation: The nuclear matrix or the nucleoplasm contains nucleolus and chromatin. The nucleoli are spherical structures present in the nucleoplasm.

questions.question: Lysosomes are rich in hydrolytic enzymes such as:
questions.question_raw: Lysosomes are rich in hydrolytic enzymes such as:
questions.explanation: Lysosomes are found to be very rich in almost all types of hydrolytic enzymes (hydrolases - lipases, proteases, carbohydrases) optimally active at the acidic pH. These enzymes are capable of digesting carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids.

questions.question: Mitochondria is called Powerhouse of the cell because:
questions.question_raw: Mitochondria is called Powerhouse of the cell because:
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are called as powerhouse of the cell because it contains an enzyme for cellular respiration in which energy is released in the form of ATP.

tests.testPaperName: Cell The Unit Of Life Test-03
tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Cell The Unit of Life

questions.question: The inner membrane of mitochondria thrown into folds to form finger-like structure is called ________.
questions.question_raw: The inner membrane of mitochondria thrown into folds to form finger-like structure is called ________.
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are double membranous structure. The inner membrane of mitochondria thrown into folds to form finger-like structure is called cristae.

questions.question: The staining procedure is developed by:
questions.question_raw: The staining procedure is developed by:
questions.explanation: The staining procedure developed by Gram viz., those that take up the gram stain are gram-positive and the others that do not are called gram-negative bacteria.

questions.question: SER differ from RER in:
questions.question_raw: SER differ from RER in:
questions.explanation: Smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER) differs from the rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER) in absence of ribosome on the surface of the endoplasmic reticulum.

questions.question: Golgi apparatus is the site for the formation of:
questions.question_raw: Golgi apparatus is the site for the formation of:
questions.explanation: Golgi apparatus is the important site of formation of glycoproteins and glycolipids.

questions.question: Elaioplasts belongs to:
questions.question_raw: Elaioplasts belongs to:
questions.explanation:
The leucoplasts are the colourless plastids of varied shapes and sizes with stored nutrients. Leucoplast includes:
 Amyloplasts storing carbohydrates.
 Elaioplasts storing oils and fats.
 Aleuroplasts storing proteins.

questions.question: How much percent of vacuoles occupy the volume of the cell in plant cells?
questions.question_raw: How much percent of vacuoles occupy the volume of the cell in plant cells?
questions.explanation: In a mature cell, the vacuole may occupy as much as 90 percent of the cell volume, such that the rest of the cell contents are flattened against the cell membrane.

questions.question: Chlorophyll is located inside_______.
questions.question_raw: Chlorophyll is located inside_______.
questions.explanation: The chloroplast consists of a number of organized flattened membranous sacs called the thylakoids, Chlorophyll pigments are present in the thylakoids.

questions.question: Which one is present nearest to plasmamembrane?
questions.question_raw: Which one is present nearest to plasmamembrane?
questions.explanation: The cell wall of a young plant cell, the primary walls capable of growth, which gradually diminishes as the cell matures and the secondary wall is formed on the inner (towards membrane) side of the cell.

questions.question: The cytoskeleton is made of:
questions.question_raw: The cytoskeleton is made of:
questions.explanation: An elaborate network of filamentous proteinaceous structures present in the cytoplasm is collectively referred to as the cytoskeleton.

questions.question: The movement of ions against the concentration gradient is
questions.question_raw: The movement of ions against the concentration gradient is
questions.explanation: Active transport is the movement of molecules across a cell membrane in the direction against their concentration gradient, i.e. moving from an area of lower concentration to an area of higher concentration.

questions.question: The chromatin contains some basic proteins are called as ________.
questions.question_raw: The chromatin contains some basic proteins are called as ________.
questions.explanation: Histone proteins the basic packer and arranger of chromatin.

questions.question: The centrioles are absent in______.
questions.question_raw: The centrioles are absent in______.
questions.explanation: Animal cells have centrioles which are absent in almost all plant cells.

questions.question: The ribosomes of a polysome translate:
questions.question_raw: The ribosomes of a polysome translate:
questions.explanation: Several ribosomes may attach to a single mRNA and form a chain called polyribosomes or polysome. The ribosomes of a polysome translate the mRNA into proteins.

questions.question: Middle lamella is composed of:
questions.question_raw: Middle lamella is composed of:
questions.explanation: The middle lamella is a layer mainly of calcium pectate which holds or glues the different neighbouring cells together.

questions.question: Plasmodesmata take parts in:
questions.question_raw: Plasmodesmata take parts in:
questions.explanation: The plasmodesmata consist of pores, or channels, lying between individual plant cells, and connect the symplastic space in the plant. They can also be termed as "bridges" between two plant cells. The plasmodesmata separate the outer cell membranes of the plant cells. A plasmodesma (plural plasmodesmata, singular plasmodesma) is a channel through the cell wall that allows molecules and substances to move back and forth as needed.

tests.testPaperName: Cell The Unit Of Life Test-04
tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Cell The Unit of Life

questions.question: Who was the first to explain that cells divide?
questions.question_raw: Who was the first to explain that cells divide?
questions.explanation: The German doctor Rudolf Virchow proposed in 1855 that all cells result from the division of previously existing cells, and this idea became a key piece of modern cell theory.

questions.question: Which of the following statements with reference to the cytoskeleton is not correct?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following statements with reference to the cytoskeleton is not correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Cytoskeleton maintains the shape of the cell

options.label: B
options.text: Cytoskeleton helpful in production of ATP

correct_option_texts: Cytoskeleton helpful in production of ATP
questions.explanation: The cytoskeleton in a cell is involved in functions like mechanical support, motility, maintenance of the shape of the cell.

questions.question: The organelle involved in respiration is_______.
questions.question_raw: The organelle involved in respiration is_______.
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are the sites of aerobic respiration. They produce cellular energy in the form of ATP.

questions.question: Cell organelles with a single membrane are ________.
questions.question_raw: Cell organelles with a single membrane are ________.
questions.explanation: Lysosomes are single membrane structures containing enzymes for digestion of all types of macromolecules.

questions.question: The site for the light reactions is ________.
questions.question_raw: The site for the light reactions is ________.
questions.explanation: In-plant cells, the chloroplasts are responsible for trapping light energy essential for photosynthesis. The grana, in the plastid, is the site of light reactions.

questions.question: Select the incorrect matching.
questions.question_raw: Select the incorrect matching.
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains the chromosomes which in turn contain the genetic material, DNA.

questions.question: The stacks of closely packed thylakoids are called ________.
questions.question_raw: The stacks of closely packed thylakoids are called ________.
questions.explanation: Granum: A stacked membranous structure within the chloroplasts of plants and green algae that contains chlorophyll and is the site of the light reactions of photosynthesis. The saclike membranes that makeup grana are known as thylakoids.

questions.question: Which one of the following is common between Prokaryotic and Eukaryotic cell?
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following is common between Prokaryotic and Eukaryotic cell?
questions.explanation: Most prokaryotic cells, particularly the bacterial cells, have a chemically complex cell envelope. All eukaryotic cells are not identical. Plant and animal cells are different as the former possess cell walls.

questions.question: The structure of the plasma membrane fluid mosaic model is proposed by:
questions.question_raw: The structure of the plasma membrane fluid mosaic model is proposed by:
questions.explanation: The fluid mosaic model explains various observations regarding the structure of functional cell membranes. The model, which was devised by SJ Singer and GL Nicolson in 1972, describes the cell membrane as a two-dimensional liquid that restricts the lateral diffusion of membrane components.

questions.question: Proteins synthesized by ribosomes on the endoplasmic reticulum are modified in________.
questions.question_raw: Proteins synthesized by ribosomes on the endoplasmic reticulum are modified in________.
questions.explanation: The cisternae of the Golgi apparatus is responsible for transporting, modifying, and packaging proteins and lipids into vesicles for delivery to targeted destinations.

questions.question: Subunits of 80S ribosome are:
questions.question_raw: Subunits of 80S ribosome are:
questions.explanation:
Each ribosome consists of two subunits.
Eukaryotes have 80S ribosomes, each consisting of a small (40S) and large (60S) subunit.
Their 40S subunit has an 18S RNA (1900 nucleotides) and 33 proteins.
The large subunit is composed of a 5S RNA (120 nucleotides), 28S RNA (4700 nucleotides), a 5.8S RNA (160 nucleotides) subunits, and 46 proteins.

questions.question: Cell drinking is another name of _______.
questions.question_raw: Cell drinking is another name of _______.
questions.explanation: Cell drinking is another name of pinocytosis. Pinocytosis, a process by which liquid droplets are ingested by living cells. Pinocytosis is one type of endocytosis, the general process by which cells engulf external substances, gathering them into special membrane-bound vesicles contained within the cell.

questions.question: Which one is not a part of the Golgi complex?
questions.question_raw: Which one is not a part of the Golgi complex?
questions.explanation: Golgi complex or Golgi body consists of cisternae, tubules and spherical vesicles. Glycosome is not present in the Golgi complex.

questions.question: Lysosomes are called suicide bag because it contain:
questions.question_raw: Lysosomes are called suicide bag because it contain:
questions.explanation: Lysosome is called a suicide bag because it contain a large number of digestive enzyme that can digest the whole cell in case of rapture.

questions.question: Which is not a function of the vacuole in a plant cell?
questions.question_raw: Which is not a function of the vacuole in a plant cell?
questions.explanation: Plant cell contains large central vacuole which helps in waste disposal, cell elongation and storage but not involved in the formation of H_2O_2.

tests.testPaperName: Cell The Unit Of Life Test-05
tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Cell The Unit of Life

questions.question: The ribosome is granules formed of:
questions.question_raw: The ribosome is granules formed of:
questions.explanation: A ribosome is a small, dense granular particle comprising usually three or four ribosomal RNA molecules and more than 50 protein molecules, interconnected to form the site of protein synthesis.

questions.question: A clear zone around Golgi apparatus is zone of _______.
questions.question_raw: A clear zone around Golgi apparatus is zone of _______.
questions.explanation: The clear zone surrounding the Golgi apparatus is a zone of exclusion, where no other cellular activity takes place.

questions.question: Largest organelles in the cell are:
questions.question_raw: Largest organelles in the cell are:

options.label: C
options.text: Mitochondria in animal cell and chloroplast in plant cell

options.label: D
options.text: Centrioles in animals and plastids in plant cell

correct_option_texts: Mitochondria in animal cell and chloroplast in plant cell
questions.explanation: Mitochondria is the largest cell organelles in animal cell and chloroplast is the largest cell organelles in a plant cell.

questions.question: A cell membrane shows fluid behaviour due to:
questions.question_raw: A cell membrane shows fluid behaviour due to:

options.label: B
options.text: Sandwich of protein between lipids layer

questions.explanation: A cell membrane shows fluid behaviour due to the mosaic of lipid and proteins. Fluid mosaic model of the plasma membrane is the most accepted and recent model.

questions.question: The structural element of chromatin is _________.
questions.question_raw: The structural element of chromatin is _________.
questions.explanation: Chromatin is the thread-like structure present in the nucleus. Chromatin has beads like structure called nucleosome that contain histone protein and DNA.

questions.question: Two cell organelles in plant cell having their own genetic material are called ________.
questions.question_raw: Two cell organelles in plant cell having their own genetic material are called ________.
