 Nutrient
 Absorbable Forms
 (i) Carbohydrate
 (a) Amino acids
 (ii) Nucleic acid
 (b) Monosaccharides
 (iii) Proteins
 (c) Sugar and bases
 (iv) Lipids
 (d) Fatty acids and glycerol
questions.question_raw:
Match the following:

Nutrient Absorbable Forms
(i) Carbohydrate (a) Amino acids
(ii) Nucleic acid (b) Monosaccharides
(iii) Proteins (c) Sugar and bases
(iv) Lipids (d) Fatty acids and glycerol
questions.explanation: Carbohydrate is converted into monosaccharides, the nucleic acid is converted into sugar and bases by nucleosidases, proteins are converted into amino acids by dipeptidases and lipids are converted into fatty acids and glycerol by lipases.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Digestion and Absorption (FA ONLY)

questions.question: Which layer of the alimentary canal is formed of loose connective tissues?
questions.question_raw: Which layer of the alimentary canal is formed of loose connective tissues?
questions.explanation: The submucosa consists of a dense and irregular layer of loose connective tissue with blood vessels, lymphatics, and nerves branching into the mucosa and muscular layer. It contains the submucous plexus, an enteric nervous plexus, situated on the inner surface of the muscular layer.

questions.question: The enzyme which hydrolyzes the starch is_______.
questions.question_raw: The enzyme which hydrolyzes the starch is_______.
questions.explanation: The enzymes that break down or hydrolyze starch into the constituent sugars are known as amylases. Alpha-amylases are found in plants and in animals. Human saliva is rich in amylase, and the pancreas also secretes the enzyme.

questions.question:
What should be filled in to replace the question mark in the following sequence:
?$\to$Trypsinogen $\to$Trypsin
questions.question_raw:
What should be filled in to replace the question mark in the following sequence:
?{tex}\to{/tex}Trypsinogen {tex}\to{/tex}Trypsin
questions.explanation: Trypsinogen is activated by an enzyme, enterokinase, secreted by the intestinal mucosa into active trypsin.

questions.question: The arrows represent :[upward to downward]
questions.question_raw: The arrows represent :[upward to downward]

options.label: C
options.text: Gall bladder and common bile duct

questions.explanation: The figure shows the muscular sac called the gall bladder. The duct of gall bladder (cystic duct) along with the hepatic duct from the liver forms the common bile duct.

questions.question: The secretions released into small intestine are:
questions.question_raw: The secretions released into small intestine are:
questions.explanation: The bile, pancreatic juice, and intestinal juice are the secretions released into the small intestine.

questions.question: Which layer of alimentary canal forms crypts in between the base of villi?
questions.question_raw: Which layer of alimentary canal forms crypts in between the base of villi?
questions.explanation: Mucosa forms gastric glands in the stomach and crypts in between the bases of villi in the intestine (crypts of Lieberkuhn).

questions.question: The optimum pH meant for hydrolyzing starch is______.
questions.question_raw: The optimum pH meant for hydrolyzing starch is______.
questions.explanation: Amylase is an enzyme that catalyses the hydrolysis of starch into sugars. Amylase is present in the saliva of humans and some other mammals, where it begins the chemical process of digestion. it is a major digestive enzyme, and its optimum pH is 6.8

questions.question: An active form of which enzyme hydrolyses milk protein in the stomach?
questions.question_raw: An active form of which enzyme hydrolyses milk protein in the stomach?
questions.explanation: Chymosin, known also as rennin, is a proteolytic enzyme related to pepsin that synthesized by chief cells in the stomach of some animals. Its role in digestion is to curdle or coagulate milk in the stomach, a process of considerable importance in the very young animal.

questions.question: The correct sequence of layers in the gut wall from inside to outside is :
questions.question_raw: The correct sequence of layers in the gut wall from inside to outside is :
questions.explanation: Serosa is the outermost layer while mucosa is the innermost layer. The layer next to mucosa is the sub-mucosa containing nerves, blood, and lymph vessels. Muscularisis found between sub-mucosa and serosa.

questions.question: One of the gut hormones which is often referred to as the hunger hormone since it works as a strong stimulant for appetite and feeding is:
questions.question_raw: One of the gut hormones which is often referred to as the hunger hormone since it works as a strong stimulant for appetite and feeding is:
questions.explanation: Ghrelin the hunger hormone is a peptide hormone produced by ghrelinergic cells in the gastrointestinal tract which function as a neuropeptide in the central nervous system. It stimulates appetite and feeding.

questions.question: Numerous folds present on the inner surface of the stomach are called rugae. Another part of the digestive tract which contains rugae is______.
questions.question_raw: Numerous folds present on the inner surface of the stomach are called rugae. Another part of the digestive tract which contains rugae is______.
questions.explanation: Numerous folds present on the inner surface of the stomach are called rugae. They are a series of ridges produced by the folding of the wall of an organ. Another part of the digestive tract which contains rugae is the palate, the top part of the inside mouth.

questions.question: Which of the following is not secreted from its source in the form of a Zymogen?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is not secreted from its source in the form of a Zymogen?
questions.explanation: Zymogen is an inactive enzyme precursor that requires a biochemical change to become active. The amylase enzyme is not released from its source in the form of a proenzyme or zymogen.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a part of the human alimentary canal?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is not a part of the human alimentary canal?
questions.explanation: The Ilium is the uppermost and largest bone of the pelvis invertebrates. Duodenum jejunum and colon are parts of the human alimentary canal but ileum is not a part of this system.

questions.question: The correct sequence of lipid digestion in alimentary canal is :
questions.question_raw: The correct sequence of lipid digestion in alimentary canal is :
questions.explanation: Digestion of (lipid) fat begins in small intestine by secretion of bile juice that contains bile salts. Bile salt emulsify the lipids into globules that is further simplified into micelles and chylomicrons.

questions.question: In elephant and walrus, projecting tusks are:
questions.question_raw: In elephant and walrus, projecting tusks are:
questions.explanation: The projecting tusk of elephant and walrus are incisors and canines respectively. These teeth are not used for chewing food.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Digestion and Absorption (FA ONLY)

questions.question:
Which of the following statement is/ are incorrect for the Enteric nervous system:
 neurons of Myentric plexus regulates glandular secretions in the GI tract.
 neurons of Meissner’s plexus regulates peristaltic waves.
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following statement is/ are incorrect for the Enteric nervous system:

  1. neurons of Myentric plexus regulates glandular secretions in the GI tract.
  2. neurons of Meissner’s plexus regulates peristaltic waves.

options.label: C
options.text: ii is correct and i is incorrect

options.label: D
options.text: i is correct and ii is incorrect

questions.explanation:
The inner plexus is the main control for GI secretion and local blood flow. The myenteric plexus is principally responsible for the peristaltic movement of the bowels. It extends throughout the entire length of the gut as a linear chain of interconnected neurons.
The Meissner or submucosal plexus regulates the configuration of the luminal surface, controls glandular secretions, alters electrolyte and water transport, and regulates local blood flow.

questions.question: Every cell contains DNA and when we consume plant/animal material, the DNA which reaches our digestive tract is digested by:
questions.question_raw: Every cell contains DNA and when we consume plant/animal material, the DNA which reaches our digestive tract is digested by:

options.label: A
options.text: Enzymes present in bile juice and gastric juice

options.label: B
options.text: Enzymes present in bile juice and succusentericus

options.label: C
options.text: Enzymes present in pancreatic juice and succus entericus

options.label: D
options.text: Enzymes present in pancreatic juice and gastric juice

correct_option_texts: Enzymes present in pancreatic juice and succus entericus
questions.explanation: Food material consists of plant or animal cells. Each cell contains DNA in the nucleus. Digestion of DNA takes place in the small intestine by the action of enzyme succus entricus.

questions.question: The process by which small electrolytes are absorbed:
questions.question_raw: The process by which small electrolytes are absorbed:
questions.explanation: Small amounts of monosaccharides like glucose, amino acids, and some of the electrolytes like chloride ions are generally absorbed by simple diffusion.

questions.question: That part of alimentary canal which is responsible for absorption of water, simple sugars, and alcohol:
questions.question_raw: That part of alimentary canal which is responsible for absorption of water, simple sugars, and alcohol:
questions.explanation:
Absorption in different parts of the digestive system: 
 The small intestine is the primary site for the absorption of water, simple sugars (like glucose), and alcohol into the bloodstream.
 The stomach absorbs very little, though alcohol and some drugs can be absorbed here.
 The large intestine primarily absorbs water and electrolytes, but not simple sugars or alcohol.
 The mouth is involved in digestion but does not play a significant role in absorption.

questions.question: Select which one is correctly matched pair:
questions.question_raw: Select which one is correctly matched pair:

options.label: A
options.text: Glucose - absorbed by passive transport that doesn’t need energy

options.label: B
options.text: Amino acids - transported by simple diffusion that doesn’t need energy

options.label: C
options.text: Fructose - transported by facilitated diffusion that doesn’t need energy

options.label: D
options.text: Galactose - absorbed by active transport that doesn’t need energy

correct_option_texts: Fructose - transported by facilitated diffusion that doesn’t need energy
questions.explanation: Some of the substances like fructose and some amino acids are absorbed with the help of the carrier ions like sodium ions. This mechanism is called facilitated transport.

questions.question: Absorption of food takes place in stomach as well as intestine but villi are present in intestine only because:
questions.question_raw: Absorption of food takes place in stomach as well as intestine but villi are present in intestine only because:

options.label: A
options.text: The stomach is already provided with microvilli

options.label: B
options.text: Intestine receives better blood supply than other parts of the alimentary canal

options.label: C
options.text: Food is converted into absorbable form within the intestine

options.label: D
options.text: The stomach is not much provided with microvilli

correct_option_texts: Food is converted into absorbable form within the intestine
questions.explanation: Villi increase the surface area thereby increasing the absorption of food. The villi also contain lymph vessels for absorbing the products of fat-digestion. From the bloodstream, the absorbed food is finally delivered to each and every cell of the body. The mucosal walls of the stomach form irregular fold known as rugae. These help in increasing the surface area to volume ratio of the expanding stomach.

questions.question: Absorption of fats takes place through:
questions.question_raw: Absorption of fats takes place through:
questions.explanation: Lymph is the extracellular fluid that constitutes the lymphatic system in the human body. The absorption of fats takes place through lymphatic ducts. As fats cannot be carried through narrow capillaries.

questions.question:
Trans fats:
 they are found in most processed and fast foods for eg. French fries.
 they help food stay fresh for a long time.
 they are a much better type of fat than saturated fats.
questions.question_raw:
Trans fats:

  1. they are found in most processed and fast foods for eg. French fries.
  2. they help food stay fresh for a long time.
  3. they are a much better type of fat than saturated fats.

options.label: B
options.text: Only i and iii are correct

options.label: C
options.text: Only i is the correct statement

options.label: D
options.text: Only i and ii are correct

correct_option_texts: Only i and ii are correct
questions.explanation: Trans fats are hydrogenated fats which make the oil more solid. They are found in most processed and fast foods. They help food stay fresh for a long time.

questions.question:
Read the following statements:
 In ruminants, cellulose digesting bacteria are present in the digestive system.
 Such digestion by microbial activity is called Anaerobic digestion.
 Camels are ruminants lacking the omasum chamber.
questions.question_raw:
Read the following statements:

  1. In ruminants, cellulose digesting bacteria are present in the digestive system.
  2. Such digestion by microbial activity is called Anaerobic digestion.
  3. Camels are ruminants lacking the omasum chamber.
questions.explanation:
In ruminants like cows, buffalos, cellulose digesting bacteria are present in the digestive system that release cellulose enzymes.
 Anaerobic digestion is a collection of processes by which microorganisms break down biodegradable material in the absence of oxygen.
 Camel is a pseudo ruminant as it has only 3 chambers instead of 4.

questions.question: If an imaginary food contains 5 grams of protein, 4 grams of fat and 10 grams of carbohydrates. Now calculate total calories it would provide ( given 1g protein = 4 calories, 1g fat = 10 calories, 1g carbohydrates = 4 calories):
questions.question_raw: If an imaginary food contains 5 grams of protein, 4 grams of fat and 10 grams of carbohydrates. Now calculate total calories it would provide ( given 1g protein = 4 calories, 1g fat = 10 calories, 1g carbohydrates = 4 calories):
questions.explanation: Five gram of protein will produce 5 $\times $ 4 = 20 calories, 4 g of fat = 4 $\times $ 10 = 40 calories and 10 g of carbohydrates = 10 $\times $ 4 = 40 calories. So total energy produced is equal to 20 + 40 + 40 = 100 calories.

questions.question: Correct sequence of layers in gut wall from inside to outside is:
questions.question_raw: Correct sequence of layers in gut wall from inside to outside is:
questions.explanation: The wall of the alimentary canal is made of four layers performing different functions. The innermost layer is called mucosa followed by submucosa,muscularis and serosa. These layers are made of different kind of tissues to perform a different function.

questions.question: The reflex action of vomiting is controlled by ______.
questions.question_raw: The reflex action of vomiting is controlled by ______.
questions.explanation: Vomiting is believed to be controlled by two distinct brain centres - the vomiting centre and the chemoreceptor trigger zone - both located in the medulla oblongata. The vomiting centre initiates and controls the act of emesis, which involves a series of contractions of the smooth muscles lining the digestive tract.

questions.question: Which digestive disorder reduces the absorption of food?
questions.question_raw: Which digestive disorder reduces the absorption of food?
questions.explanation: The abnormal frequency of bowel movement and increased liquidity of the fecal discharge reduces the absorption of food leading to diarrhoea.

questions.question: Which one is a mismatched pair?
questions.question_raw: Which one is a mismatched pair?
questions.explanation: The abnormal frequency of bowel movement and increased liquidity of the faecal discharge is known as diarrhoea.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Biology > Digestion and Absorption (FA ONLY)

questions.question:
Match the following:
 Digestive Disorder
 Cause
 (a) Constipation
 (i) gallstones
 (b) Jaundice
 (ii) reflex action anxiety
 (c) Indigestion
 (iii) intentional ischemia
 (d) Vomiting
 (iv) irregular bowel movements
questions.question_raw:
Match the following:

Digestive Disorder Cause
(a) Constipation (i) gallstones
(b) Jaundice (ii) reflex action anxiety
(c) Indigestion (iii) intentional ischemia
(d) Vomiting (iv) irregular bowel movements
questions.explanation: The causes of indigestion are inadequate enzyme secretion, anxiety, food poisoning, overeating, and spicy food. In constipation, the bowel movements occur irregularly. In jaundice, the deposition of bile pigments results in gall stones. A reflex action is controlled by the vomiting centre in the medulla.

questions.question: Malnutrition covers:
questions.question_raw: Malnutrition covers:

options.label: B
options.text: All the diseases which infants suffer from

questions.explanation: Malnutrition is the condition of having less than the required amount of food or more than the required amount of food. Both conditions are harmful to health. So, malnutrition covers both undernutrition and overnutrition.

questions.question: Bacteria responsible for chronic gastritis is:
questions.question_raw: Bacteria responsible for chronic gastritis is:
questions.explanation: Chronic gastritis involves swelling or inflammation of the stomach lining. Helicobacter pylori colonize the stomach and induce chronic gastritis, a long-lasting inflammation of the stomach.

questions.question: Regurgitation is more common in infants than in adults. The reason will be:
questions.question_raw: Regurgitation is more common in infants than in adults. The reason will be:

options.label: A
options.text: Pyloric sphincter is much less effective in infants

options.label: B
options.text: Excessive acid formation and secretion in the stomach of infants

options.label: C
options.text: Cardiac sphincter is much less effective in infants

options.label: D
options.text: Peristaltic waves are not strong enough in infants

correct_option_texts: Cardiac sphincter is much less effective in infants
questions.explanation: Regurgitation is the expulsion of material from the pharynx, or esophagus, usually characterized by the presence of undigested food or blood. Regurgitation is used by a number of species to feed their young. Regurgitation is more common in infants than adults because the cardiac sphincter is much less effective in infants.

questions.question: ________ from pharynx is conveyed into oesophagus by ________.
questions.question_raw: ________ from pharynx is conveyed into oesophagus by ________.
questions.explanation: Bolus from pharynx is passed into the oesophagus by deglutition. The bolus is the semisolid chewed food mixed with saliva in the mouth. The food from pharynx passed to the oesophagus by swallowing.

questions.question:
Read the following statements about CCK:
 This hormone stimulates gastrointestinal motility and pancreatic endocrine function.
 This hormone stimulates release of bile from the gall bladder.
questions.question_raw:
Read the following statements about CCK:

  1. This hormone stimulates gastrointestinal motility and pancreatic endocrine function.
  2. This hormone stimulates release of bile from the gall bladder.

options.label: B
options.text: Statement ii is correct and i is wrong

options.label: C
options.text: Statement ii is wrong and i is correct

correct_option_texts: Statement ii is correct and i is wrong
questions.explanation:
Cholecystokinin, previously called pancreozymins, is synthesized and secreted by enteroendocrine cells in the duodenum, the first segment of the small intestine.
Its presence causes the release of digestive enzymes and bile from the pancreas and gallbladder, respectively, and also acts as a hunger suppressant.

questions.question:
People who are lactose intolerant:
 Cannot digest food with lactose in them.
 They need to take a calcium supplement.
questions.question_raw:
People who are lactose intolerant:

options.label: A
options.text: Statement ii is correct and i is wrong

options.label: B

questions.explanation: People who are lactose intolerant cannot digest food with lactose in them and these people need to take a calcium supplement which is naturally present along with lactose in milk.

questions.question: Liver stores extra glycogen but once storage limit is reached then extra carbohydrate will be:
questions.question_raw: Liver stores extra glycogen but once storage limit is reached then extra carbohydrate will be:
questions.explanation: Glycogen is the form in which the body stores glucose. The liver can only store about 100 g of glucose in the form of glycogen. The muscles also store glycogen. Muscles can store approximately 500 g of glycogen. Because of the limited storage areas, any carbohydrates that are consumed beyond the storage capacity are converted to and stored as fat. There is practically no limit on how many calories the body can store as fat.

questions.question: In Reema’s body, substances in bile harden forming stone in the gall bladder. What can be the possible treatment?
questions.question_raw: In Reema’s body, substances in bile harden forming stone in the gall bladder. What can be the possible treatment?

options.label: B
options.text: Removing only stone from gall bladder by surgery

options.label: D
options.text: Dissolving the stone with medicinal treatment

questions.explanation: The substance in bile hardens to form stone in the gall bladder. It is difficult to remove stone surgically and by lithotripsy. So, in most cases, the gall bladder is removed surgically.

questions.question: Which of the following is a mismatched pair?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is a mismatched pair?
questions.explanation: Diastema is the gap between teeth. Generally, a diastema is not present in human beings. Rabbit has rootless teeth and frogs are acrodont as same teeth remain throughout their life.

questions.question:
Blood vessels of the alimentary canal:
 provide nourishment to the cells of the alimentary canal.
 absorb digested fat molecules from the stomach and intestine.

Blood vessels of the alimentary canal:

  2. absorb digested fat molecules from the stomach and intestine.

options.label: B
options.text: Statement i is wrong and ii is correct

options.label: C
options.text: Statement i is correct and ii is wrong

questions.explanation: Blood vessels of the alimentary canal provide nourishment to the cells of the alimentary canal but do not absorb digested fat molecules from the stomach and intestine.

questions.question: Out of the following which one doesn’t provide any calories?
questions.question_raw: Out of the following which one doesn’t provide any calories?
questions.explanation: Fats, protein, and alcohol on oxidation produce energy along with other products but vitamins are not oxidized to release energy to us. They act as protective food to protect us from the disease.

 Function

questions.question: Parodontax toothpaste launched a few months ago that helps to prevent periodontal disease which are caused by microbe named:
questions.question_raw: Parodontax toothpaste launched a few months ago that helps to prevent periodontal disease which are caused by microbe named:
questions.explanation: Porphyromonas gingivalis is a Gram-negative oral anaerobe that is involved in the pathogenesis of periodontitis, an inflammatory disease that destroys the tissues supporting the tooth which eventually may lead to tooth loss. Paodontax toothpaste help to prevent periodontal disease.

questions.question: Tracheal tube divides into right and left bronchi at the level of:
questions.question_raw: Tracheal tube divides into right and left bronchi at the level of:
questions.explanation: The trachea divides at the level of 5th thoracic vertebra into a right and left primary bronchi.

questions.question: Vital capacity of our lungs include all of the following except:
questions.question_raw: Vital capacity of our lungs include all of the following except:
questions.explanation: Vital capacity includes ERV, TV, and IRV or the maximum volume of air a person can breathe out after a forced inspiration.

questions.question: Skin is the only respiratory organ in:
questions.question_raw: Skin is the only respiratory organ in:
questions.explanation: There are no special respiratory organs in Hirudinaria. In addition to its primary role as a protective covering, the skin also serves a respiratory function. It is provided with a rich supply of haemocoelomic fluid in an extensive system of capillaries and capillary loops, which penetrate into the spaces between the inner ends of the epidermal cells.

questions.question: The pharynx is the common passage for food and air but still, food does not enter into the windpipe due to the presence of _______.
questions.question_raw: The pharynx is the common passage for food and air but still, food does not enter into the windpipe due to the presence of _______.
questions.explanation: The epiglottis is a leaf-shaped flap of cartilage located behind the tongue, at the top of the larynx, or voice box. The main function of the epiglottis is to seal off the windpipe during eating, so that food is not accidentally inhaled.

questions.question: The value of TV is about 500 ml for an average adult human male but only some parts of it enter the lung alveoli and the rest of it fills the respiratory passage. What should be the distribution ratio for alveolar volume /respiratory passage volume?
questions.question_raw: The value of TV is about 500 ml for an average adult human male but only some parts of it enter the lung alveoli and the rest of it fills the respiratory passage. What should be the distribution ratio for alveolar volume /respiratory passage volume?
questions.explanation:
Tidal volume (TV) = volume moved in one breath. Normal = 500 ml
 About 70% of TV reaches alveoli (350 ml) Only this amount is involved in gas exchange
 30% in airways =respiratory passage (anatomical dead space)
So ratio = 350:150 = 7:3

questions.question: It is known that exposure to carbon monoxide is harmful to animals because
questions.question_raw: It is known that exposure to carbon monoxide is harmful to animals because
questions.explanation: Carbon monoxide poisoning occurs after enough inhalation of carbon monoxide (CO). Carbon monoxide binds with haemoglobin to form carboxyhaemoglobin because of which oxygen is not released to the tissues.

questions.question: Ram suffers punctures in his chest cavity without any damage to the lungs in an accident. It results in:
questions.question_raw: Ram suffers punctures in his chest cavity without any damage to the lungs in an accident. It results in:
questions.explanation: Haemopneumothorax is a medical term describing the combination of two conditions: pneumothorax, or air in the chest cavity, and hemothorax (also called haemothorax), or blood in the chest cavity. A hemothorax, pneumothorax, or both can occur if the chest wall is punctured. Because of the punctured chest wall, the lungs cannot expand, the pressure in the lungs never drops and no air is pulled into the bronchi. Respiration is not possible.

questions.question: Which one of the following is the wrong statement with respect to the functions of the respiratory system?
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following is the wrong statement with respect to the functions of the respiratory system?
questions.explanation: The function of the respiratory system does not deal with the cleaning up of the air.

questions.question:

 Respiratory volume
 Value (in ml)

 (b) ERV
 (ii) 6000 - 8000
 (c) TV
 (iii) 2500 - 3000
 (d) IRV

questions.question_raw:

Respiratory volume Value (in ml)
(a) RV (i) 1000 - 1100
(b) ERV (ii) 6000 - 8000
(c) TV (iii) 2500 - 3000
(d) IRV (iv) 1100 - 1200
questions.explanation: TV is approx. 500 mL., i.e., a healthy man can inspire or expire approximately 6000 to 8000mL of air per minute. IRV averages 2500mL to 3000 mL. ERV averages 1000mL to 1100 mL. RV averages 1100 mL to 1200 mL.

questions.question: Respiration in insects is direct due to exchange of gases:
questions.question_raw: Respiration in insects is direct due to exchange of gases:

options.label: B
options.text: By tracheal tubes directly with haemocoel which then exchanges with tissues

options.label: C
options.text: Directly with the air in tubes

options.label: D
options.text: Directly with the air outside through body surface

correct_option_texts: By tracheal tubes directly with haemocoel which then exchanges with tissues
questions.explanation: Insects have spiracles on their exoskeletons to allow air to enter the trachea. In insects, the tracheal tubes primarily deliver oxygen directly into the insects' tissues. The spiracles can be opened and closed in an efficient manner to reduce water loss. This is done by contracting closer muscles surrounding the spiracle.

questions.question: If TLC is 5500ml, IRV is 2950ml, ERV is 900ml and TV is 500ml then what will be the value of RV?
questions.question_raw: If TLC is 5500ml, IRV is 2950ml, ERV is 900ml and TV is 500ml then what will be the value of RV?
questions.explanation:
RV = TLC - IRV - ERV - TV
substitute the values
RV = 5500 - 2950 - 900 - 500 = 1150ml.

questions.question:
The correct sequence of respiration is:
 Breathing in oxygen.
 Transportation of gases by the blood.
 Diffusion of gases between blood and tissues.
 Diffusion of gases across the alveolar membrane.
 Using oxygen and releasing carbon dioxide.
questions.question_raw:
The correct sequence of respiration is:

  1. Breathing in oxygen.
  2. Transportation of gases by the blood.
  3. Diffusion of gases between blood and tissues.
  4. Diffusion of gases across the alveolar membrane.
  5. Using oxygen and releasing carbon dioxide.
questions.explanation: Respiration involves breathing or pulmonary ventilation by which atmospheric air is drawn in. Diffusion of gases across the alveolar membrane, Transport of gases by the blood, Diffusion of gases between blood and tissues. The utilization of oxygen by the cells.

questions.question: Those bronchioles which divide into alveolar ducts are called as:
questions.question_raw: Those bronchioles which divide into alveolar ducts are called as:
questions.explanation: Terminal bronchioles give rise to respiratory bronchioles that ultimately lead to the alveoli. The exchange of gases takes place in alveoli with blood vessels.

questions.question: Which part of our respiratory system doesn’t need ciliated epithelium and thus have non-ciliated walls?
questions.question_raw: Which part of our respiratory system doesn’t need ciliated epithelium and thus have non-ciliated walls?
questions.explanation: The respiratory system does not need ciliated epithelium and thus have non-ciliated walls such as alveoli. Alveoli are the balloon-like structure at which the exchange of gases takes place.

questions.question: After a forceful expiration:
questions.question_raw: After a forceful expiration:

options.label: B
options.text: Diaphragm and intercostals increase respiratory surface area

options.label: D
options.text: Lungs becomes empty so exchange stops for a while

questions.explanation: After a forceful expiration, the gaseous exchange continues uninterrupted because there is always a residual volume of air that continues the diffusion of gases.

questions.question: What is the difference between the two given below diagrams:

questions.explanation: Figure (i) represents the alveoli of healthy person and figure (ii) is the alveoli of emphysema patient. The wall of alveoli is get damaged in figure (ii) to reduce the surface area for exchange of gases.

questions.question: First step in the process of inspiration is:
questions.question_raw: First step in the process of inspiration is:

options.label: B
options.text: Increase in volume of thoracic cavity

questions.explanation: Inspiration is the process of intake of oxygen from the surrounding. This is achieved by increasing the volume of the thoracic cavity. The contraction of the diaphragm relaxes the sternum that increases the thoracic cavity.

questions.question: The gaseous exchange portion of the human respiratory tract includes :
questions.question_raw: The gaseous exchange portion of the human respiratory tract includes :

options.label: A
options.text: alveoli, bronchioles, trachea and nasal chambers

questions.explanation: Alveolar ducts are tiny ducts that connect the respiratory bronchioles to alveolar sacs, each of which contains a collection of alveoli (small mucus-lined pouches made of flattened epithelial cells). They are tiny end ducts of the branching airways that fill the lungs.

questions.question: Air breathing animals lose a large amount of precious water from their respiratory surface through:
questions.question_raw: Air breathing animals lose a large amount of precious water from their respiratory surface through:
questions.explanation: Water is released in the atmosphere along with expired air during the respiratory cycle. Air-breathing animals lose a large amount of precious water from their respiratory surface through evaporation.

questions.question: Which of the following pair of factors are considered favourable for the formation of oxyhaemoglobin?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following pair of factors are considered favourable for the formation of oxyhaemoglobin?

options.label: A
options.text: High H^+ concentration and high pO_2

options.label: C
options.text: Low pO_2 and low H^+ concentration

options.label: D
options.text: Low H^+ concentration and high pCO_2

questions.explanation: In the alveoli, where there is high pO_2, low pCO_2, lesser H^+ concentration, and lower temperature, the factors are all favourable for the formation of oxyhaemoglobin.

questions.question:
Read the following statements:
 Pressure contributed by and individual gas in a mixture of gases is called its partial pressure.
 pCO_2 in alveolar air is 45 mm Hg and in venous blood is 40 mm Hg.
questions.question_raw:
Read the following statements:

  1. Pressure contributed by and individual gas in a mixture of gases is called its partial pressure.
  2. pCO2 in alveolar air is 45 mm Hg and in venous blood is 40 mm Hg.

options.label: B
options.text: Statement (i) is wrong and (ii) is correct

options.label: C
options.text: Statement (i) is correct and (ii) is wrong

correct_option_texts: Statement (i) is correct and (ii) is wrong
questions.explanation:
In a mixture of gases, each gas has a partial pressure which is the hypothetical pressure of that gas if it alone occupied the entire volume of the original mixture at the same temperature. The total pressure of an ideal gas mixture is the sum of the partial pressures of each individual gas in the mixture.
 The pCO_2 in alveolar air is 40 mm Hg and in venous blood is 45 mm Hg.

questions.question: The principle of exchange of gases at the lungs is:
questions.question_raw: The principle of exchange of gases at the lungs is:
questions.explanation: The main function of the respiratory system is the gaseous exchange. This refers to the process of Oxygen and Carbon Dioxide moving between the lungs and blood. Diffusion occurs when molecules move from an area of high concentration (of that molecule) to an area of low concentration. This occurs during the gaseous exchange as the blood in the capillaries surrounding the alveoli has a lower oxygen concentration of Oxygen than the air in the alveoli which has just been inhaled.

questions.question:

 The affinity of Hb for CO is 200 to 250 times more than its affinity for oxygen therefore CO competes with oxygen for binding to Hb.
 The result of this binding is a shift of oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve to the left and its alteration to a more hyperbolic shape.

  1. The affinity of Hb for CO is 200 to 250 times more than its affinity for oxygen therefore CO competes with oxygen for binding to Hb.

options.label: A
options.text: Statement (i) is incorrect and (ii) is correct

questions.question:

 Positive intrapulmonary pressure
 Negative intrapulmonary pressure
 Less pressure within lungs as compared to atmospheric pressure
questions.question_raw:

  2. Negative intrapulmonary pressure

questions.explanation: Expiration is the release of carbon dioxide-rich air from the thoracic cavity. When the volume of air decreases and pressure increase the air from the chest moves out. This kind of pressure is called positive intrapulmonary pressure.

questions.question:

 RBCs are permanently anaerobic and muscle cells are temporarily anaerobic.
 RBCs are temporarily anaerobic and muscle cells are permanently anaerobic.

  2. Haemocyanin is the pigment present in the blood of Palaemon and is copper-containing protein.
  3. Haemocyanin is much more efficient than haemoglobin.
questions.explanation: Haemoglobin is the pigment present in our blood and is iron-containing protein. Haemocyanin is the pigment present in the blood of Palaemon and is copper-containing protein.

questions.question:
The given graph shows $ {O_2}$ consumption and ventilation during exercise so we can infer from this graph :
During exercise, oxygen consumption rate in our body increases.
Neural stimulus allows abdominal muscles, which are a kind of smooth muscle to contract to facilitate ventilation while exercising.
Increase in ventilation during exercise prevents large changes in the partial pressure of $ {O_2}$ or $ C{O_2}$
The overall impact is the breathing rate increases.
questions.question_raw:
The given graph shows {tex} {O_2}{/tex} consumption and ventilation during exercise so we can infer from this graph :

  1. During exercise, oxygen consumption rate in our body increases.
  2. Neural stimulus allows abdominal muscles, which are a kind of smooth muscle to contract to facilitate ventilation while exercising.
  3. Increase in ventilation during exercise prevents large changes in the partial pressure of {tex} {O_2}{/tex} or {tex} C{O_2}{/tex}
  4. The overall impact is the breathing rate increases.

options.label: A
options.text: all the above statements are correct.

questions.explanation: During exercise Oxygen consumption and ventilation changes drastically. Oxygen consumption rate in our body increases during exercise. Increase in ventilation during exercise prevents a large change in the partial pressure of O_2 and CO_2 to increase breathing rate.

questions.question:
The exchange of gases in:
 The alveoli that lower the pO_2 of blood and raise its pCO_2 is external respiration.
 The tissues that lower the pCO_2 of blood and raise its pO_2 is internal respiration.
questions.question_raw:
The exchange of gases in:

  1. The alveoli that lower the pO2 of blood and raise its pCO2 is external respiration.
  2. The tissues that lower the pCO2 of blood and raise its pO2 is internal respiration.

options.label: C
options.text: Statement (ii) is incorrect and (i) is correct

options.label: D
options.text: Statement (i) is incorrect and (ii) is correct

questions.explanation: In the alveoli, there is high pO_2, low pCO_2, lesser H^+ concentration, and lower temperature.

questions.question: Which of the following is not correctly matched?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is not correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Respiration is a physiological process. Total lung capacity (TLC) includes vital capacity (VC) and residual volume. Cutaneous respiration is breathing via the skin.

questions.question:
Read the following statements:
 Every 100 ml of deoxygenated blood delivers about 4 ml of CO_2 to the alveoli.

 At the respiratory surface, CO_2 diffuses from venous blood into alveolar spaces.

Read the following statements:

  1. Every 100 ml of deoxygenated blood delivers about 4 ml of CO2 to the alveoli.
  2. 30% - 40% of CO2 is transported to the alveoli as carbamino-hemoglobin.
  3. At the respiratory surface, CO2 diffuses from venous blood into alveolar spaces.
questions.explanation: 20 - 25% of CO_2 is transported to the alveoli as carbamino-hemoglobin.

questions.question: The condition favourable for dissociation of oxygen from oxyhemoglobin in the tissues is:
questions.question_raw: The condition favourable for dissociation of oxygen from oxyhemoglobin in the tissues is:

options.label: A
options.text: Low pO_2, high pCO_2, low H^+ concentration, and higher temperature

options.label: B
options.text: High pO_2, low pCO_2, high H^+ concentration, and lower temperature

options.label: C
options.text: High pO_2, low pCO_2, low H^+ concentration, and lower temperature

options.label: D
options.text: Low pO_2, high pCO_2, high H^+ concentration, and higher temperature

correct_option_texts: Low pO_2, high pCO_2, high H^+ concentration, and higher temperature
questions.explanation: In tissues, low pO_2, high pCO_2, high H^+ concentration, and higher temperature exist, the conditions are favourable for dissociation of oxygen from the oxyhaemoglobin.

questions.question: The solubility of carbon dioxide is:
questions.question_raw: The solubility of carbon dioxide is:

options.label: A
options.text: 10 - 15 times higher than that of oxygen

options.label: B
options.text: 20 - 25 times lower than that of oxygen

options.label: C
options.text: 10 - 15 times lower than that of oxygen

options.label: D
options.text: 20 - 25 times higher than that of oxygen

correct_option_texts: 20 - 25 times higher than that of oxygen
questions.explanation: The solubility of carbon dioxide is 20-25 times higher than that of oxygen, the amount of carbon dioxide that can diffuse through the diffusion membrane per unit difference in partial pressure is much higher compared to that of oxygen.

questions.question: The major factor which affects binding related to the partial pressure of carbon dioxide is______.
questions.question_raw: The major factor which affects binding related to the partial pressure of carbon dioxide is______.
questions.explanation: pCO_2

questions.question: 70% of carbon dioxide is transported as bicarbonate by the enzyme_______.
questions.question_raw: 70% of carbon dioxide is transported as bicarbonate by the enzyme_______.
questions.explanation: An enzyme present in red blood cells, carbonic anhydrase, aids in the conversion of carbon dioxide to carbonic acid and bicarbonate ions. When red blood cells reach the lungs, the same enzyme helps to convert the bicarbonate ions back to carbon dioxide, which we breathe out. It transports nearly 70% of carbon dioxide as bicarbonate.

questions.question: The regulatory centres for respiration are located in:
questions.question_raw: The regulatory centres for respiration are located in:
questions.explanation: A specialized centre present in the medulla region of the brain called respiratory rhythm centre is primarily responsible for this regulation. In the pons region of the brain called pneumotaxic centre can moderate the functions of the respiratory rhythm centre.

questions.question: In CNS, regulation of respiratory rhythm during normal breathing is largely dependent upon:
questions.question_raw: In CNS, regulation of respiratory rhythm during normal breathing is largely dependent upon:
questions.explanation: In the central nervous system, regulation of respiratory rhythm during normal breathing is dependent upon the concentration of carbon dioxide gas in the air.

questions.question: Name the unlabelled parts of a given flow chart:
questions.question_raw: Name the unlabelled parts of a given flow chart:

options.label: B
options.text: oxygen, inspiratory centre and expiratory centre

options.label: C
options.text: stretch, expiratory centre and inspiratory centre

correct_option_texts: stretch, expiratory centre and inspiratory centre
questions.explanation: The flow chart of respiratory mechanism labeled as stretch, expiratory centre and inspiratory centre respiratory. It is controlled by the medulla and pons of hind brain.

questions.question: The specialized centre which can reduce the inspiratory duration upon stimulation is_________.
questions.question_raw: The specialized centre which can reduce the inspiratory duration upon stimulation is_________.
questions.explanation: The pneumotaxic centre can moderate the functions of the respiratory rhythm centre. A neural signal from this centre can reduce the duration of inspiration and thereby alter the respiratory rate.

questions.question: Loud snoring and labored breathing are symptoms of:
questions.question_raw: Loud snoring and labored breathing are symptoms of:
questions.explanation: Loud snoring and labored breathing are the symptoms of obstructive sleep apnea. Obstructive sleep apnea is a sleep disorder in which breathing is briefly and repeatedly interrupted.

questions.question: A film of ________ lines lung alveoli that lowers ________ of the alveoli and makes breathing ________.
questions.question_raw: A film of ________ lines lung alveoli that lowers ________ of the alveoli and makes breathing ________.
questions.explanation: A thin film of lecithin lines lung alveoli that lowers the surface tension of the alveoli and makes breathing easier. The lining also reduces the friction of alveoli to avoid its damage.

questions.question: Habit of Cigarette smoking can lead to:
questions.question_raw: Habit of Cigarette smoking can lead to:

options.label: A
options.text: Loss of cilia lining the respiratory tract

questions.explanation: The habit of cigarette smoking can lead to coughing, emphysema, and loss of cilia lining the respiratory tract. It contains nicotine that breaks the wall of alveoli to reduce the respiratory surface.

questions.question:
Read the following:
 During chloride shift, the chloride ions diffuse from RBC’s into the blood plasma.
 The chloride shift helps to maintain ionic balance and chemical neutrality.
 The chloride shift occurs during the transport of CO_2.
questions.question_raw:
Read the following:

  1. During chloride shift, the chloride ions diffuse from RBC’s into the blood plasma.
  2. The chloride shift helps to maintain ionic balance and chemical neutrality.
  3. The chloride shift occurs during the transport of CO2.
questions.explanation: Chloride shift is a process that occurs in a cardiovascular system and refers to the exchange of bicarbonate and chloride across the membrane of red blood cells. The chloride shift helps to maintain ionic balance and chemical neutrality.

questions.question: Insect of the family Chironomidae like Chironomous larvae are also known as bloodworms since:
questions.question_raw: Insect of the family Chironomidae like Chironomous larvae are also known as bloodworms since:

options.label: B

options.label: C
options.text: They contain haemoglobin as their respiratory pigment

correct_option_texts: They contain haemoglobin as their respiratory pigment
questions.explanation: Insects of family Chironomidae eg. Chironomus commonly known as the harlequin fly is a species of the non-biting midge. Their larvae are known by the common name of blood worm due to their red colouration. The red colouration is because they contain haemoglobin as their respiratory pigment.

questions.question: Which of the following blood components play a major role in blood coagulation?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following blood components play a major role in blood coagulation?
questions.explanation: Platelets also called thrombocytes are cell fragments produced from megakaryocytes. Platelets can release a variety of substances most of which are involved in the coagulation or clotting of blood.

questions.question: The pre-hypertension blood pressure value is a measurement between:
questions.question_raw: The pre-hypertension blood pressure value is a measurement between:
questions.explanation: Hypertension or high blood pressure is a condition where blood pressure increases substantially. The pre-hypertension blood pressure value is between 120/80 and 139/89.

questions.question: Where (a) is a neutrophil, identify the other four WBCs?
questions.question_raw: Where (a) is a neutrophil, identify the other four WBCs?
questions.explanation: There are two main categories of WBCs – granulocytes and agranulocytes. Neutrophils, eosinophils and basophils are different types of granulocytes, while lymphocytes and monocytes are the agranulocytes.

questions.question: The middle man of our body is:
questions.question_raw: The middle man of our body is:
questions.explanation: Lymph is the extracellular fluid that contains WBC’s and plasma. Lymph flows in the lymphatic system and finally drains into veins. Lymph is called as the middle man of our body.

questions.question_raw: Correct order for increasing number of corpuscles in our blood is:
questions.explanation: WBCs are relatively lesser in number which averages 6000-8000 mm^-3 of blood. A healthy adult man has, on an average, 5 million to 5.5 million of RBCs mm-3 of blood. Blood normally contains 1,500,00-3,500,00 platelets mm^-^3.

questions.question: Among mammals, the nucleus is absent in:
questions.question_raw: Among mammals, the nucleus is absent in:
questions.explanation: The absence of a nucleus is an adaptation of the red blood cell for its role. It allows the red blood cell to contain more hemoglobin and, therefore, carry more oxygen molecules. Platelets are devoid of the nucleus in most of the mammals.

options.label: B

In our blood:

questions.explanation: 90-92 per cent of plasma is water and proteins contribute 6-8 per cent of it. Plasma also contains small amounts of minerals and other materials such as glucose, amino acids, lipids, etc., arealso present in the plasma.

questions.question: When blood clot starts contracting, a pale yellow fluid starts oozing out. Its name and composition are:
questions.question_raw: When blood clot starts contracting, a pale yellow fluid starts oozing out. Its name and composition are:
questions.explanation: Plasma without the clotting factors is called serum. The clotting factors include fibrinogen and blood corpuscles.

questions.explanation: Tissue fluid (lymph) and blood plasma represent the extracellular fluids. Lymph flows in lymphatic vessels and plasma in arteries and veins along with RBC, WBC, and platelets.

questions.question: The specialized patch of modified heart muscles from where contraction initiates, is/are:
questions.question_raw: The specialized patch of modified heart muscles from where contraction initiates, is/are:
questions.explanation: The nodal musculature has the ability to generate action potentials without any external stimuli. The number of action potentials that could be generated in a minute varies at different parts of the nodal system. The SAN can generate the maximum number of action potentials and is responsible for initiating and maintaining the rhythmic contractile activity of the heart. Therefore, it is called the pacemaker.

questions.question: The incorrect statement about the human heart is:
questions.question_raw: The incorrect statement about the human heart is:

options.label: C

questions.explanation: Human being is triploblastic. The different organs are evolved from different layers of foetus. Human heart is derived from mesoderm.

questions.question: The layer of heart pericardium which checks it's overstretching and overfilling as well as also protects the heart from mechanical injury is:
questions.question_raw: The layer of heart pericardium which checks it's overstretching and overfilling as well as also protects the heart from mechanical injury is:
questions.explanation: The pericardium is a protective covering of the heart. The pericardium is made of different layers to perform different functions. The layer of heart pericardium which checks it's overstretching and overfilling as well as protects the heart from mechanical injury is the fibrous pericardium.

 Blood vessels include arteries and veins. Arteries carry blood from the heart to body parts and veins bring back blood from body parts to the heart.
 The blood vessel which carry blood from the heart to lungs is a pulmonary artery and it carries oxygenated blood.
questions.question_raw:

  1. Blood vessels include arteries and veins. Arteries carry blood from the heart to body parts and veins bring back blood from body parts to the heart.
  2. The blood vessel which carry blood from the heart to lungs is a pulmonary artery and it carries oxygenated blood.

options.label: B
options.text: Statement (ii) is incorrect and (i) is correct

options.label: C
options.text: Statement (ii) is correct and (i) is incorrect

correct_option_texts: Statement (ii) is incorrect and (i) is correct
questions.explanation: Blood vessels include arteries and veins. Arteries carry blood from the heart to body parts and veins bring back blood from body parts to the heart. The pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements doesn’t hold true for blood clotting cascade?
 Ruptured platelets release thromboplastin.
 Prothrombinis converted into thrombin in the presence of vitamin K and Na^+ions.
 Fibrinogen is converted into fibrin through enzymatic action of thrombin in the presence of Ca^++ions.
 Fibrin makes clot via process of polymerisation.
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following statements doesn’t hold true for blood clotting cascade?

  1. Ruptured platelets release thromboplastin.
  2. Prothrombinis converted into thrombin in the presence of vitamin K and Na+ions.
  3. Fibrinogen is converted into fibrin through enzymatic action of thrombin in the presence of Ca++ions.
  4. Fibrin makes clot via process of polymerisation.
questions.explanation: Ruptured platelets release thromboplastin. Fibrinogen present in blood plasma is converted into fibrin through enzymatic action of thrombin. Fibrin makes clot via process of polymerisation.

questions.question:

 The instrument used for recording the heart’s electrical events is named electrocardiogram.
 In any ECG the part of the baseline between any two deflections is called an interval.
 Abnormality in the working of heart changes the wave pattern of ECG.
questions.question_raw:
Read the following:

  1. The instrument used for recording the heart’s electrical events is named electrocardiogram.
  2. In any ECG the part of the baseline between any two deflections is called an interval.
  3. Abnormality in the working of heart changes the wave pattern of ECG.
questions.explanation: ECG is a graphical representation of the electrical activity of the heart during a cardiac cycle.

questions.question: The mass of tissue seen in the left corner of the right atrium close to the atri-ventricular septum is_____.
questions.question_raw: The mass of tissue seen in the left corner of the right atrium close to the atri-ventricular septum is_____.
questions.explanation: Atrioventricular Node or AV node is a compact half oval mass of myogenic fibres which lies in the posterior septal wall of the right atrium near the opening of the coronary sinus. It receives an impulse from the SA node and transmits it to ventricles.

questions.question: Look at the following ECGs:
questions.question_raw: Look at the following ECGs:

options.label: A
options.text: (ii) ECG is abnormal while (i) is a normal ECG

options.label: B
options.text: (ii) represents enlargement of atrium and (i) represents enlargement of ventricles

options.label: D
options.text: (i) represents enlargement of atrium and (ii) represents enlargement of ventricles

questions.explanation: Both electro cardiogram (ECG) represents myocardial infarction in which heart do not receive the sufficient oxygen due to blockage of coronary artery.

questions.question: Blood pressure is expressed as the ratio of systolic over diastolic pressure. The difference between systolic and diastolic pressure is called pulse pressure. What will be its value for a normal healthy adult?
questions.question_raw: Blood pressure is expressed as the ratio of systolic over diastolic pressure. The difference between systolic and diastolic pressure is called pulse pressure. What will be its value for a normal healthy adult?
questions.explanation: Pulse pressure is the difference between the systolic and diastolic pressure readings. It is measured in millimeters of mercury (mmHg). It represents the force that the heart generates each time it contracts. If resting blood pressure is (systolic-diastolic) 120-80 millimeters of mercury (mmHg), the pulse pressure is 40.

questions.question:
In the human heart:
 The valve between the right atrium and right ventricle is tricuspid while the valve between the left atrium and left ventricle is the mitral valve.
 Openings of the right and the left ventricles into the pulmonary artery and the aorta are guarded by semilunar valves.
 ‘Lub’ the first sound which is low pitched is caused by the closure of semilunar valves while ‘Dub’ the second sound which is high pitched is caused by the closure of bicuspid and tricuspid valves.
questions.question_raw:
In the human heart:

  1. The valve between the right atrium and right ventricle is tricuspid while the valve between the left atrium and left ventricle is the mitral valve.
  2. Openings of the right and the left ventricles into the pulmonary artery and the aorta are guarded by semilunar valves.
  3. ‘Lub’ the first sound which is low pitched is caused by the closure of semilunar valves while ‘Dub’ the second sound which is high pitched is caused by the closure of bicuspid and tricuspid valves.
questions.explanation: The first heart sound (lub) is associated with the closure of the tricuspid and bicuspid valves whereas the second heart sound (dub) is associated with the closure of the semilunar valves.

questions.question: Each cardiac cycle takes 0.8 seconds to occur. Calculate how many cardiac cycles occur in 4 minutes?
questions.question_raw: Each cardiac cycle takes 0.8 seconds to occur. Calculate how many cardiac cycles occur in 4 minutes?
questions.explanation: 4 min = 240 sec. Therefore, 240/0.8 = 300 cardiac cycles.

questions.question: The left atrium receives deoxygenated blood in:

questions.question:

 When the heart is not pumping blood sufficient to meet the need of the body then this state is called a heart attack.
 When the heart stops beating due to the irreversible death of cells then this state is called heart failure.

questions.explanation: The state when the heart is not pumping blood sufficient to meet the need of the body then this state is called heart failure. When the heart muscles are suddenly damaged due to inadequate blood supply is called a heart attack.

questions.question: In the systemic circulation, blood vessel that carries blood from the intestine to liver is named:
questions.question_raw: In the systemic circulation, blood vessel that carries blood from the intestine to liver is named:
questions.explanation: The hepatic portal vein carries blood from intestine to the liver before it is delivered to the systemic circulation.

questions.question: The cardiovascular centre is located in:
questions.question_raw: The cardiovascular centre is located in:
questions.explanation: The cardiovascular centre is a part of the human brain responsible for the regulation of the rate at which the heart beats through the nervous and endocrine systems. It is found in the medulla oblongata.

questions.question:
Prothrombin plays a crucial role in blood coagulation and vitamin K is considered essential for blood clotting, now read the following:
 Vitamin K is necessary for the synthesis of prothrombin in the liver.
 Vitamin K is necessary for the synthesis of prothrombin in the bone marrow.
 Vitamin K is necessary for the conversion of prothrombin into active thrombin.
questions.question_raw:
Prothrombin plays a crucial role in blood coagulation and vitamin K is considered essential for blood clotting, now read the following:

  1. Vitamin K is necessary for the synthesis of prothrombin in the liver.
  2. Vitamin K is necessary for the synthesis of prothrombin in the bone marrow.
  3. Vitamin K is necessary for the conversion of prothrombin into active thrombin.
questions.explanation: Prothrombin plays a crucial role in blood coagulation and vitamin K is essential for blood clotting. Vitamin K is necessary for the synthesis of prothrombin in the liver.

questions.question: Mayank stood still for a long time and he felt swelling of feet. What would you suggest to him?
questions.question_raw: Mayank stood still for a long time and he felt swelling of feet. What would you suggest to him?

options.label: B
options.text: Keep on standing and do some shoulder exercise

options.label: C
options.text: Ask for some medicine to cure his swelling of feet

options.label: D
options.text: Jerk or wriggle his legs to restart circulation

correct_option_texts: Jerk or wriggle his legs to restart circulation
questions.explanation: During long hours of standing still swelling of feet is observe due to the accumulation of blood in the lower limb due to the gravitation effect. This can be corrected by a jerk or wriggle of legs to restart circulation properly.

questions.question: Angina pectoris is very often mistaken for:
questions.question_raw: Angina pectoris is very often mistaken for:
questions.explanation: Angina pectoris is the sensation of chest pain, pressure, or squeezing or pain in the centre of the chest. These symptoms are often mistaken by indigestion.

questions.question: The list of risk factors associated with hypertension doesn’t include:
questions.question_raw: The list of risk factors associated with hypertension doesn’t include:
questions.explanation: Hypertension can cause a number of effects on the functioning of different vital functions but hypertension does not associate with hypoglycemia and diabetes insipidus.

questions.question:

questions.question:
When a person suffers from chest pain:
 the reason for this suffering is decreased blood flow and simultaneously lowered oxygen supply to an area of the heart.

options.label: D

correct_option_texts: Statement (i) is correct and (ii) is incorrect
questions.explanation: Statement (i) is correct and (ii) is incorrect

questions.question: As Soham, ages with chronic CAD the lumen of his coronary artery may get nearly completely obstructed Result is flow of blood to heart tissue is restricted such that the tissue receives an inadequate supply of oxygen-rich blood and this condition is diagnosed as:
questions.question_raw: As Soham, ages with chronic CAD the lumen of his coronary artery may get nearly completely obstructed Result is flow of blood to heart tissue is restricted such that the tissue receives an inadequate supply of oxygen-rich blood and this condition is diagnosed as:
questions.explanation: In case of chronic CAD, the lumen of coronary artery may get nearly completely obstructed that results into restricted flow of blood to heart. The tissue receives inadequate bloods called myocardial ischemia.

questions.question: Meghna suffers from allergic asthma. After a blood test, her leucocyte count displayed an abnormal increase in the number of:
questions.question_raw: Meghna suffers from allergic asthma. After a blood test, her leucocyte count displayed an abnormal increase in the number of:
questions.explanation: An absolute eosinophil count is a blood test that measures the number of white blood cells called eosinophils. Eosinophils become active when you have certain allergic diseases infections and other medical conditions.

questions.question: For a healthy individual, calculate stroke volume for him if other values are; heartbeat per minute = 72 and cardiac output is 5 litre?
questions.question_raw: For a healthy individual, calculate stroke volume for him if other values are; heartbeat per minute = 72 and cardiac output is 5 litre?
questions.explanation: E Stroke volume of a healthy individual can be calculated by dividing cardiac output by a number of heartbeat per minute. So stroke volume = 5/72 = 0.0694.

questions.question: Sarla is on a protein-deficient diet. As a result, her plasma proteins content is reduced. She may develop symptom like:
questions.question_raw: Sarla is on a protein-deficient diet. As a result, her plasma proteins content is reduced. She may develop symptom like:
questions.explanation: Consuming a protein-deficient diet for longer duration may result in a reduction in protein plasma reduction. The symptoms that appear on the body include swelling of hands and feet.

options.label: A

questions.explanation: Amphibians have arteriovenous heart and two accessory chambers. Fishes have two-chambered venous heart and two accessory chambers. In mammals, complete double circulation with a four-chambered heart without any accessory chambers.

Animal Circulation method

chapters.name: Excretory Products and their Elimination
